column3: David and the commanders of the army set apart for service the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, who were to prophesy with lyres, harps, and cymbals. Lapide notes that the author of Chronicles (identified by the Rabbis, Lyra, Sixtus of Siena, Driedo, and others as Ezra) compiled this book from public annals and diaries, supplying what was omitted in the Books of Kings, as Jerome wrote to Domnius. While much is repeated from Kings, what is distinctive here is treated more fully or explained more clearly than in the parallel passages.

Among the sons of Asaph: Zaccur, Joseph, Nethaniah, and Asarelah — sons of Asaph, under the direction of Asaph, who prophesied under the king's supervision. Lapide observes that prophesying here refers to sacred song and musical performance in divine worship, a form of inspired praise rather than prediction of future events.

Of Jeduthun: his sons Gedaliah, Zeri, Jeshaiah, Shimei, Hashabiah, and Mattithiah — six in number — under the direction of their father Jeduthun, who prophesied with the lyre in thanksgiving and praise to the Lord.

Of Heman: his sons Bukkiah, Mattaniah, Uzziel, Shebuel, Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, Romamti-ezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, and Mahazioth. Lapide notes the remarkable number of Heman's sons — fourteen — as a sign of divine blessing upon the chief singer of the sanctuary.

All these were sons of Heman the king's seer, given by God's promise to exalt him, for God had given Heman fourteen sons and three daughters. Lapide remarks that Heman is called the king's seer (videns regis) because he received divine revelations and communicated them through sacred music, and that the three daughters joined in the liturgical singing, a notable instance of women's participation in temple worship.

All these were under the direction of their fathers for the singing in the house of the Lord, with cymbals, harps, and lyres, for the service of the house of God — Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman under the authority of the king. Lapide emphasizes the hierarchical order David established, with everything subordinated to royal authority, reflecting the divinely sanctioned structure of sacred worship.

Their number, together with their relatives who were trained in singing to the Lord — all who were skilled — was 288. Lapide notes that this large company of trained musicians testifies to David's extraordinary zeal for the dignity of divine worship, organizing sacred music with the same care he devoted to every other aspect of temple service.

They cast lots for their duties, small and great alike, teacher and pupil together. Lapide notes that the use of lots here signified that the division of liturgical offices was committed to divine providence rather than human preference or favoritism, so that no one could claim superiority or complain of injustice in the assignment of sacred duties.

The first lot fell to Asaph's family, to Joseph; the second to Gedaliah, together with his brothers and sons — twelve in all. Lapide explains that twenty-four divisions of singers were established corresponding to the twenty-four divisions of priests instituted by David, so that the entire liturgical order of the temple mirrored a perfect, divinely ordered symmetry in both priesthood and song.

The third lot fell to Zaccur, his sons and his brothers — twelve. Lapide observes that throughout this chapter the consistent number twelve for each division signifies completeness and order, evoking the twelve tribes of Israel and pointing to the comprehensive scope of worship offered on behalf of the whole people of God.

The twenty-fourth lot fell to Romamti-ezer, his sons and his brothers — twelve. Lapide concludes his commentary on this chapter by noting that the full catalogue of twenty-four divisions of singers, each of twelve men, yields a total of 288 trained musicians (as stated in v. 7), all serving in a carefully ordered rotation that David established under divine inspiration to ensure that the praise of God would be offered continually and worthily in the temple he had prepared for the Lord.

Lapide notes that the catalogues of genealogies and persons which are read at length in the first ten chapters are similar to other such catalogues of genealogies and persons found elsewhere in Scripture.

The first ten chapters contain nothing but genealogies, because the Hebrews were scrupulous about these for two reasons: the distinction of the twelve tribes, and on account of Christ, so that it might be established that He was born of Abraham, David, and the tribe of Judah. Because these ten chapters have limited practical use and record only names of those begotten, Lapide treats them briefly. Note that proper names of men and women in this book are often different from the names given to the same persons in Genesis and other books, because the Hebrews were polyonymous (having multiple names). For example, the sons of Benjamin listed in Genesis 46:21 differ from those listed here.

St. Jerome, in his letter to Paulinus prefixed to the Bible, says of Chronicles: "The book of Paralipomenon, that is, the epitome of the Old Testament, is of such great importance that whoever wishes to claim knowledge of the Scriptures without it only mocks himself. For in its individual names and junctions of words, histories passed over in the books of Kings are touched upon, and innumerable questions of the Gospel are resolved." This book is therefore an epitome or compendium of the deeds of the Kings of Judah — not of the Kings of Israel, who were schismatics and idolaters. In Hebrew it is titled 'Dibre Haiyamim,' meaning 'words of the days,' that is, a diary or chronicle briefly narrating the deeds of David and the subsequent Kings of Judah, as if day by day, that is, through individual times and ages. The Septuagint titles the book 'Paraleipomenon,' meaning 'things left over' or 'remaining,' as St. Jerome states.
Cush begot Nimrod, who began to be mighty on the earth. This verse is noted for its brief characterization of Nimrod as the first to establish earthly dominion.
The sons of Shem are enumerated here: Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, Aram, Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech — the ancestors of the Semitic peoples from whom the line leading to Abraham descends.

To Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg, because in his days the earth was divided, and the name of his brother was Joktan. The division of the earth refers to the confusion of tongues at Babel and the subsequent dispersion of peoples.
Abram — this is Abraham. The Chronicler notes the identity of Abram and Abraham, the same person whose name God changed, signifying his role as father of many nations.
The sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael. Isaac is placed first, though Ishmael was the firstborn, because Isaac was the son of promise through whom the covenant line continues.

Abraham begot Isaac, whose sons were Esau and Israel. Israel (Jacob) is named by his honorific name received at the wrestling with the angel, signifying the chosen line through whom the twelve tribes descend.

These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before a king reigned over the sons of Israel. The listing of Edomite kings before Israel had any king is noted as remarkable — that a gentile nation had monarchical government prior to God's chosen people, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy that Esau's descendants would also possess dominion (Gen. 27:40).
After the death of Adad, chiefs replaced kings in Edom. The transition from kings to dukes (chiefs) in Edom is recorded, listing the various clan leaders who governed in place of monarchs.

Adam, Seth, Enos, Cainan, etc. — that is to say: Adam begat Seth, Seth begat Enos, Enos begat Cainan, and so on in sequence for the rest whom the text lists. For the sake of brevity, the verb "begat" is omitted so that it need not be repeated for each individual; thus only the names of the fathers and sons are set down.
Noah — understand "begat" — "Shem, Ham and Japheth," as was explained in Genesis 6 and following chapters, where all these matters were treated in full.
Keturah is called a concubine here not in the sense of a mistress, but of a secondary wife who was not the mistress of the household; hence her sons did not succeed to the father's inheritance.

Concerning "Timna, Amalek": some hold that Timna is in the genitive case, because Genesis 36:12 says that Esau begat Amalek from Timna his concubine. But the Timna here is a different person from the Timna of Genesis; for that Timna was the concubine of Esau, whereas this one is his daughter or son, as is clear from the Hebrew and Septuagint, which read: "and Timna, and Amalek."

The sons of Seir: this Seir is distinct from Esau, who was likewise surnamed Seir (meaning "hairy") because he was shaggy. For Esau was the son of Abraham and Isaac, and his sons — different from the sons of this Seir — were listed in verse 35. This Seir, however, was not a descendant of Abraham and Isaac, but was a Horite by race who occupied Edom before Esau, and his genealogy is given in Genesis 36:20. He is introduced here solely so that the lineage of Timna, from whom Amalek was born, might be known.

These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom — with scepter, diadem, and royal authority. Note: just as the Hebrews first had judges, as it were commanders, from Joshua to Saul, then kings from Saul to Zedekiah and the Babylonian captivity, and after that again rulers from Zerubbabel to the Maccabees; so also the Edomites first had chiefs until Bela, then kings from Bela until the death of the second Hadad, and then again chiefs from Timna to Hiram, as is clear from verses 51 and 52.
Bela the first king of Edom died, and Jobab son of Zerah reigned in his place. At that time, therefore, the kingship was not hereditary.

Lapide notes that among the kings of Edom, succession was not from father to son, but to whoever was most worthy (dignior). Thus after Bela, the first king of Idumaea, Jobab succeeded — who was the son of Zara, not of Bela.

Lapide identifies this Jobab with Job, the mirror of patience (speculum patientiae), as he discussed at Genesis 36:31. Job was therefore the second king of Idumaea; his ancestor Esau had only been a duke (dux) of that land. The lineage runs: Esau begot Raguel, Raguel begot Zara, Zara begot Jobab, that is, Job.
The sons of Judah — Her, Onan, and Shelah — were born of the daughter of Shua the Canaanite. Her, the firstborn of Judah, was evil before the Lord and God slew him.
Tamar, Judah's daughter-in-law, bore him Phares and Zara; thus all the sons of Judah were five in number.
The sons of Zara: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara — five in all.
The sons of Carmi: Achar (Achan), who troubled Israel and sinned by stealing from the anathema (the accursed thing devoted to God).
The sons of Hezron who were born to him: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Calubi (Caleb).
Ram begot Amminadab, and Amminadab begot Nahshon, prince of the sons of Judah.
Nahshon begot Salma, from whom Boaz descended.
Boaz begot Obed, who himself begot Jesse (Isai).
Jesse begot as firstborn Eliab, second Abinadab, third Shimma (Shammah).
Fourth Nathanael, fifth Raddai.
Sixth Ozem, seventh David — indicating David was the youngest of seven sons listed here.
Their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. The sons of Zeruiah: Abishai, Joab, and Asahel — three sons.
Abigail bore Amasa, whose father was Jether the Ishmaelite.
Caleb the son of Hezron took a wife named Azubah, by whom he begot Jerioth; and his sons were Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon.
When Azubah died, Caleb took Ephrath as wife, who bore him Hur.
Hur begot Uri, and Uri begot Bezaleel — the craftsman of the Tabernacle.
After this, Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead and took her when he was sixty years old; she bore him Segub.
Segub begot Jair, who possessed twenty-three cities in the land of Gilead.
Geshur and Aram took the towns of Jair, and Kenath and its villages, sixty cities in all — all these were sons of Machir the father of Gilead.
After Hezron died, Caleb went in to Ephrath. Hezron also had a wife named Abiah, who bore him Ashur the father of Tekoa.
The sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron: Ram his firstborn, and Bunah, and Aram, and Ozem, and Ahijah.
Jerahmeel also took another wife named Atarah, who was the mother of Onam.
The sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel were Maaz, Jamin, and Eker.
Onam had sons: Shammai and Jada. The sons of Shammai: Nadab and Abishur.
The name of Abishur's wife was Abihail, who bore him Ahban and Molid.
The sons of Nadab were Seled and Appaim; Seled died without children.
The son of Appaim was Ishi; Ishi begot Sheshan, and Sheshan begot Ahlai.
The sons of Jada, brother of Shammai: Jether and Jonathan. Jether also died without children.
Jonathan begot Peleth and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel.
Sheshan had no sons, but daughters; and he had an Egyptian servant named Jarha.
Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha as wife, and she bore him Attai.
Attai begot Nathan, and Nathan begot Zabad.
Zabad begot Ephlal, and Ephlal begot Obed.
Obed begot Jehu, and Jehu begot Azariah.
Azariah begot Helez, and Helez begot Eleasah.
Eleasah begot Sisamai, and Sisamai begot Shallum.
Her was the firstborn of Judah, whom God slew on account of the filth of softness (mollities) and pollution, as Lapide explains at Genesis 38:7.
Achar is the same as Achan, who troubled (turbavit) the camp of the Hebrews by his theft, and was therefore called Achar, meaning "troubler." See the commentary on Joshua 7.
Ram here is the same as Aram, father of Aminadab in Matthew 1, the name being shortened by aphaeresis to Ram.
David is called the seventh son here, whereas in 1 Kings 16:10 eight sons of Jesse are numbered. Lapide explains this discrepancy in his commentary on that passage.
From the mention of the sons of Zeruiah — Abishai, Joab, and Asahel — it appears that Abishai was senior to his brother Joab, who was commander of David's army.
Sheshan begat Ahlai as a daughter, not a son, as is clear from verse 34.

Who saw half of the resting places: Lapide offers five interpretations. (1) St. Jerome (Against the Pelagians, Book 1) says this refers to a prophet, since prophets were called "seers" (videntes), meaning those who foresaw and predicted the future — so Sobal was a seer, but only partially, seeing heavenly things (the place of rest) incompletely. (2) Vatablus takes "seeing" to mean governing or being prince over the half-district called Hammenuchoth (resting places) in Hebrew, perhaps named for the fertility and pleasantness of the land — since to "see" can mean to be a prince, as a prince surveys and oversees all his people. (3) Others take "resting place" to mean a tomb, in which the body rests after death until the general resurrection; hence Benedictus translates the parallel in v. 54 as "who possessed half of the tomb of Sarah (wife of Abraham) and of the four Patriarchs," which tomb was at Hebron. (4) Sanchez takes "resting place" to mean the region in which one dwells and quietly possesses as lord, citing Virgil's Aeneid 8 ("Hic locus urbis erit requies et certa laborum"), so that Sobal "saw," i.e., possessed and occupied with both eyes and hands, half of the region where his ancestors had quietly dwelt — a meaning paralleled in Genesis 49:15 of Issachar ("he saw rest, that it was good") and Psalm 131:8,14. (5) The fifth and most literal interpretation: "Who saw half of the resting places" is itself a proper name of Sobal's son, signifying that Sobal had two sons — Cariathiarim (so named because he dwelt in forests, Hebrew iearim) and one whose Hebrew name Imroe chasi hammenuchot means "seeing half of the resting places," so called because he possessed half of his father Sobal's resting place (i.e., heritage and region), while his brother Cariathiarim possessed the other half. This is confirmed by the Septuagint retaining these as proper Hebrew names, by Roman codices writing "Qui videbat" with a capital Q as a proper name, and by the fact that all the following terms (Coronae domus, Canentes, Resonantes, in tabernaculis commorantes, Qui venerunt de Camore) are likewise proper names. # GEM

The sons of Salma: Bethlehem, Netophathi, the Crown of the house of Joab, and the Half of the resting places of Sarai: Benedictus translates this as "who possessed half of the tomb of Sarah and of the four Patriarchs," but Lapide notes that "Sarai" here is not the same as Abraham's wife Sara, since the Hebrew spelling differs (Sarai here uses the letters Tsade and Ayin, while Sara uses Shin). The proper meaning is that Salma had four sons: (1) Bethlehem, (2) Netophathi, (3) one called in Hebrew Alerot beth Joab, i.e., Crown of the house of Joab, and (4) one called Half of the resting places of Sarai. All these are proper names, as confirmed by the Septuagint treating them as such. Why the last two were so named is uncertain — perhaps the father Salma wished the third to be the crown and glory of the house of Joab, and the fourth to occupy half the resting places (regions and possessions) of Sarai.

The families also of the scribes (skilled in the Law) dwelling in Jabez, the Singing ones, the Resounding ones, and those dwelling in tents: These are also proper names, as is clear from the Hebrew, from Roman codices which print them with capital letters, and from the Septuagint which retains and translates the Hebrew names as proper — rendering them as Tarathim, Samathim, Sachathim. The meaning is that the families (Hebrew mishpachoth) of the scribes, i.e., of the Kenites (as follows), descended from Salma were three: Tarathim (the Singing ones), Samathim (the Resounding ones), and Sachathim (those dwelling in tents) — because the true Kenites sang the praises of God.

David had sons born to him in Hebron: the firstborn Amnon, of Achinoam the Jezreelitess; the second Daniel, of Abigail the Carmelitess; the third Absalom, son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur; the fourth Adonijah, son of Haggith.
The fifth was Shephatiah, of Abital; the sixth Ithream, of Eglah his wife. These six were born to David in Hebron.
Six sons were born to him in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months. Then he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem.

In Jerusalem were born to him: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon — four sons of Bathsheba, daughter of Ammiel. Lapide notes that Bathsheba is here called daughter of Ammiel, whereas in 2 Samuel 11:3 she is called daughter of Eliam; these are likely the same person, the name being given in reversed form.
Also Ibhar, and Elishua — among the sons born to David in Jerusalem.
And Eliphelet, and Nogah, and Nepheg, and Japhia — further sons of David in Jerusalem.
Also Elishama, and Eliada, and Eliphelet — nine in total among the sons of David born in Jerusalem.

All these were sons of David, apart from the sons of the concubines; and they had a sister, Tamar. Lapide notes that this Tamar is she who was violated by her brother Amnon, as recounted in 2 Samuel 13.

The son of Solomon was Rehoboam; his son was Abijah, who begat Asa; from Asa was born Jehoshaphat. Lapide begins here the genealogical succession of the kings of Judah, noting that the Chronicler carefully preserves the royal line through which the promise to David was fulfilled.
Jehoshaphat was father of Joram; Joram begat Ahaziah, from whom was born Joash.
Amaziah, son of Joash, begat Azariah; and the son of Azariah was Jotham.
Jotham procreated Ahaz, father of Hezekiah, from whom was born Manasseh.

Manasseh begat Amon, father of Josiah. Lapide observes that this genealogical list traces the royal Davidic line continuously, demonstrating God's fidelity to His covenant with David despite the sins of individual kings.

Concerning the sons of Josiah: the firstborn was Johanan, the second Joiakim, the third Zedekiah, the fourth Shallum. Johanan died early, perhaps slain in battle with his father Josiah, so the succession went to Jehoahaz (Joachaz), then Joiakim, then Zedekiah. Jehoahaz was a man of two names, being also called Shallum, and was third-born by age. For when Jehoahaz began to reign he was 18 years old, while Joiakim who succeeded him after three months was 25, and Zedekiah who followed after eleven years of Joiakim's reign was only 21. Shallum is placed last not by age but by dignity of reign — or rather indignity — and its brevity, having reigned only three months before being taken captive by Pharaoh and led to Egypt. In order of age the sons were: 1. Johanan, 2. Joiakim, 3. Jehoahaz/Shallum, 4. Zedekiah.

The sons of Jeconiah were Assir, Shealtiel, etc. — eight sons are enumerated here, yet Jeconiah is called "childless" (sterilis) in Jeremiah 22:30, because none of them succeeded him in the kingdom, as Jeremiah explains in that same passage.

From Pedaiah were born Zerubbabel and Shimei. This Zerubbabel is a different person from the Zerubbabel who is the ancestor of Christ in Matthew 1, though he was his cousin. The Zerubbabel of Matthew 1 was the son of Shealtiel. However, some hold that there is only one Zerubbabel, who was by nature the son of Pedaiah but by adoption the son of Shealtiel. Those who hold this view must consequently say that one of the sons of Zerubbabel listed here bore another name, namely Abiud, since Matthew 1 says Zerubbabel begat Abiud. Mariana therefore conjectures that Hananiah, who follows here, was also known by the name Abiud.

Some think that the first three sons born to Bathsheba — Shimma, Shobab, and Nathan — were actually the sons of Uriah, later adopted by David to compensate for the wrong done to Uriah through adultery and murder; and that Solomon alone was David's true son by Bathsheba, since Solomon says in Proverbs 4:3 that he was "only-begotten before his mother." But others, more correctly, hold that all four were David's sons, and that Solomon is called "only-begotten" in the sense of being uniquely beloved by his mother, as Lapide explains at Proverbs 4:3.
Daughter of Ammiel: Ammiel was also called Eliam, who was the son of Ahithophel and one of the thirty mighty men (heroes) of David, as recorded in 2 Samuel 23:34.
The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal. This genealogy and the following material was explained at 2 Samuel 3:2 and following verses.

Jabez was more distinguished than his brothers, and his mother called his name Jabez, saying: "Because I bore him in pain." The name Jabez thus derives from the sorrow of his birth, yet his distinction among his brethren shows that a sorrowful name and origin does not determine one's greatness.

Jabez called upon the God of Israel, saying: "If you will indeed bless me and enlarge my territory, and your hand be with me, and you keep me from harm so that it might not bring me pain." And God granted what he asked. This is a notable example of confident prayer: Jabez asked for blessing, expansion of borders, divine assistance, and preservation from evil — and received all of it. A model of bold yet humble petition to God. # GEM
Saraiah begat Joab, father of the Valley of Craftsmen (Vallis artificum), for they were craftsmen. This indicates the hereditary transmission of skilled trades among certain families of Judah.
Among the sons of Ezra: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon; and he begat Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa.

His wife Judaiah bore Jared father of Gedor, Heber father of Socho, and Jekuthiel father of Zanoah. These are the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, whom Mered married. Bithiah, daughter of Pharaoh, is here incorporated into the genealogy of Judah through her marriage to Mered — a notable instance of a royal Egyptian woman becoming part of Israel's heritage.

The sons of Shelah son of Judah: Er father of Lecah, Laadah father of Mareshah, and the families of those who worked fine linen (byssus) in Beth-ashbea. This reference to workers of fine linen indicates that certain crafts and trades were maintained within particular family lines of Judah.

And he who caused the sun to stand still, and the men of Lying, and Secure, and Burning, who were princes in Moab, and who returned to Lahem. Now these are ancient records. This obscure verse is noted as containing old and difficult records; Lapide acknowledges the antiquity of the tradition without resolving all its difficulties.

These are the potters dwelling in Plantations and in Hedges, with the king in his works, and they dwelt there. Certain families of Judah are identified as royal craftsmen — potters who lived in settlements called Netaim (Plantations) and Gederah (Hedges), serving the king in his workshops.

The sons of Shimei were sixteen, and daughters six; but his brothers did not have many sons, and the whole family could not match the number of the children of Judah. This notes the relatively smaller population growth of the tribe of Simeon compared to Judah, a demographic disparity already visible in this early period.

The Simeonites dwelt in Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual, Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim — these were their cities until the reign of David.

Lapide refutes the Rabbinical fable (cited in connection with Matthew 1) that Elimelech was a most holy man who, seeing his fellow citizens turning to idols, asked God to stop the sun as a portent to terrify idolaters and draw them back to himself. This is shown to be a mere Rabbinical fable for two reasons: first, because the passage concerns the posterity of Shelah, whereas Elimelech was from the posterity of Phares; second, because the name Joacim (Hebrew יוֹקִים, Jokim) simply means "one who causes to stand, confirms," and some Rabbis added to the fable on the basis of this name alone.

Lapide discusses the name "Jokim," explaining that Joshua (Josue) was given this cognomen by the Hebrews because he made the sun stand still (sistens solem). Over time, "Jokim" came to signify in popular usage "he who made the sun stand still." He notes that all manuscripts include the word "solem." Lapide then refutes the Rabbinic tradition (cited by Jerome, though without approval) that Elimelech was Jokim, offering seven arguments against it: (1) Elimelech does not mean "who made the sun stand still" but "God is king"; (2) Mahlon means not "secure" but "weak and sick"; (3) Chelion means not "burning" but "completed" and "dove-like"; (4) Mahlon and Chelion never returned to Bethlehem but died in Moab; (5) the "men of lies" are distinct from Securus and Incendens; (6) Mahlon and Chelion were not princes in Moab but poor foreigners; (7) the book of Ruth, which narrates their lives in detail, is silent about any such miracle. Cajetanus, Serarius, Salianus, and Sanchez all agree. Therefore no miraculous solar standstill should be posited beyond those recorded under Joshua (Josh. 10) and Hezekiah (Isa. 36). Lapide concludes that this Jokim was likely a man of heroic deeds who was called a second Joshua — just as men today are called Hercules, Samson, Julius, Alexander, Pompey, or Scipio to honor ancient heroes, even without matching their deeds. He further notes that the Romans similarly called Jupiter "Stator" because he made fleeing Romans stand firm against the Samnites, citing Cicero and Ovid. Pagninius adds that Jokim/Joakim may be composed of an abbreviated form of Jehovah and Jakim, signifying "God establishes" or "God the Stayer" — i.e., of the sun at Joshua's prayer. # GEM

On the phrase "who returned to Lahem": Lapide explains this refers to Bethlehem, which by aphaeresis is called "Lahem" or "Lehem," citing 1 Chr. 20:5 where "Bethlehemite" appears as "Lehemites" in Hebrew. He draws parallel contractions: Aram called "Ham" (1 Chr. 2:9) and Jechonias called "Chonias" (Jer. 22:24). On "these are ancient words": Vatablus interprets this as meaning these are the ancient names of ancient heroes.

On "these are the potters dwelling in plantations and hedges with the king in his work": Vatablus explains that Nathaim and Gedera are place names derived from gardens and hedges. Lapide interprets: these were the famous potters who dwelt at Nathaim and Gedera — that is, among plants and hedges — who served in the royal gardens of King David, assisting in his royal works. Note: some incorrectly read "singuli" (individuals) instead of "figuli" (potters).

In which the sons of Ham had dwelt before — that is, the Canaanites; for these were descended from Canaan, son of Ham, son of Noah. The meaning is: the Simeonites, as the number of their tribe's children grew and they were crowded for space — especially in the midst of the tribe of Judah, which at the schism under Rehoboam reclaimed its cities from the Simeonites — for this reason invaded the neighboring Canaanites who had remained in the holy land after Joshua and could not be expelled by the Hebrews, and occupied their cities, fields, and pastures at the beginning of the reign of Hezekiah. But they enjoyed these only for a short time, for in the sixth year of Hezekiah they were led away captive along with the remaining nine tribes by Shalmaneser.

The sons, i.e., the posterity of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh are enumerated, who likewise drove out the neighboring Canaanites from their regions under King Jotham; but soon under Hezekiah they were first carried away into Assyria on account of their idolatry.

The firstborn rights of Reuben were transferred to the sons of Joseph — namely Ephraim and Manasseh — because the firstborn held a double portion of the paternal inheritance (while the other brothers each received a single share). Reuben forfeited this by violating his father's couch (cf. Gen. 35:22).

Judah is said to be the strongest among his brothers, and from his lineage princes sprang forth — referring to the kings of the house of David — yet the birthright itself was reckoned to Joseph, not to Judah.
The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel: Enoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi, as also recorded in Gen. 46 and Num. 26.
Beerah, whom Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria carried away captive; he was a prince in the tribe of Reuben. This shows that the Assyrian deportation affected even the leaders of the tribe.

Bela son of Azaz, son of Shema, son of Joel, dwelt in Aroer as far as Nebo and Baal-meon — these being cities on the eastern side of the Jordan in the land of Gilead, originally belonging to the Moabites but taken by Reuben.

He dwelt eastward as far as the entrance of the wilderness and the river Euphrates, for they possessed a great multitude of livestock in the land of Gilead — indicating the vast pastoral wealth of these trans-Jordanian tribes.

In the days of Saul they made war against the Hagrites and defeated them, and they dwelt in their tents throughout all the region east of Gilead — an early military expansion before the later census under Jotham and Jeroboam.
The sons of Gad dwelt opposite them in the land of Bashan as far as Salecah, occupying territory to the north of Reuben's allotment.
Joel was chief, and Shapham the second; then Janai and Shaphat in Bashan — these being the principal leaders among the Gadites in their territory.
Their kinsmen according to their families: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jachan, Zia, and Eber — seven in all. These are the clans of Gad enumerated by family heads.

These were the sons of Abihail son of Huri, son of Jaroah, son of Gilead, son of Michael, son of Jeshishai, son of Jahdo, son of Buz — a genealogy tracing the lineage of the Gadite chiefs back through seven generations.
And the brothers, the sons of Abdiel son of Guni, were chief of their father's house — indicating another branch of leading families among the Gadites.
They dwelt in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns, and in all the pasturelands of Sharon as far as their borders — showing the wide extent of Gadite settlement across trans-Jordan.

All of these were enrolled in the days of Jotham king of Judah and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel. The Jeroboam here is Jeroboam II, son of Joash, a powerful king of Israel, contemporary with Jotham. The census was thus conducted under two kings reigning simultaneously over the divided kingdom.

The sons of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had 44,760 warriors bearing shields and swords, drawing the bow, and trained for battle — a formidable combined military force from the trans-Jordanian tribes.

They made war against the Hagrites, and Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab gave them assistance — these latter being Arabian peoples descended from Ishmael (cf. Gen. 25:15), who aided the Hagrites against Israel.

The Hagrites were delivered into their hands, because they cried to God in the battle, and He was entreated of them, because they put their trust in Him. This is a notable testimony that military victory is granted by God to those who pray with faith — a lesson underscored by the sacred historian. # GEM
They took as spoil: 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, 2,000 donkeys, and 100,000 persons — an immense victory showing the scale of this campaign against the desert peoples east of the Jordan.

Many fell slain because the war was God's war. They dwelt in their place until the captivity — meaning they occupied the territory of the defeated Hagrites until the Assyrian deportation removed them from the land.

The sons of the half-tribe of Manasseh possessed the land from Bashan to Baal-hermon, Senir, and Mount Hermon — an extensive northern territory. Their numbers were very great, hence their need for such a wide territory.

These were the heads of their fathers' houses: Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel — mighty men of valor, famous men, and heads of their fathers' houses. The sacred writer particularly commends their valor and renown among their clans.

But they transgressed against the God of their fathers and played the harlot after the gods of the peoples of the land, whom God had destroyed before them. Their idolatry is described as spiritual fornication — a betrayal of their covenant with God who had given them victory over those very peoples whose gods they now worshipped. # GEM

God stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria and the spirit of Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria, and he carried away the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, bringing them to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river of Gozan, unto this day. God is the primary cause of the deportation — He moved the Assyrian kings as instruments of His just punishment for Israel's idolatry. Pul and Tilgath-pilneser may be the same king under two names, or two successive Assyrian monarchs.
The sons of Levi are listed as Gerson, Caath, and Merari — the three great branches of the Levitical tribe from which all priestly and temple-service families descend.
The sons of Caath are Amram, Isaar, Hebron, and Oziel — Caath being the most distinguished branch, from which both the priestly line of Aaron and the prophetic figure of Moses descend.

The sons of Amram are Aaron, Moses, and Miriam. From Aaron descend the priests: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Nadab and Abihu died offering strange fire before the Lord (Lev. 10), so the priestly succession passed through Eleazar and Ithamar.
Eleazar begat Phinehas, who is celebrated for his zeal in slaying Zimri and Cozbi (Num. 25), by which act the priesthood was confirmed to his line with a covenant of perpetual peace.
Phinehas begat Abishua, and Abishua begat Bukki — continuing the high-priestly line through the descendants of Eleazar.
Bukki begat Uzzi, and Uzzi begat Zerahiah — the genealogical succession of the high priests is carefully preserved here to show the continuity of the sacred office.
Zerahiah begat Meraioth, and Meraioth begat Amariah, and Amariah begat Ahitub — linking the earlier patriarchal priests to those who served in the period of the monarchy.
Ahitub begat Zadok, and Zadok begat Ahimaaz. Zadok is the famous high priest who remained faithful to David during the rebellion of Absalom and later anointed Solomon king.
Ahimaaz begat Azariah, and Azariah begat Johanan — continuing the unbroken descent of the high-priestly line.

Johanan begat Azariah, who exercised the priesthood in the house which Solomon built in Jerusalem. This Azariah is notable as the priest who resisted King Uzziah when he presumed to usurp the priestly office and offer incense (2 Chr. 26:17–18).
Azariah begat Amariah, and Amariah begat Ahitub — a second Ahitub in the line, not to be confused with the earlier one (v. 7), illustrating the common practice of recycling ancestral names.
Ahitub begat Zadok — a second Zadok in the priestly line — and Zadok begat Shallum.
Shallum begat Hilkiah, and Hilkiah begat Azariah. This Hilkiah is the celebrated high priest who discovered the book of the Law in the Temple during the reign of King Josiah (2 Kgs. 22:8).
Azariah begat Seraiah, and Seraiah begat Jehozadak — bringing the high-priestly genealogy down to the eve of the Babylonian exile.

Jehozadak went into exile when the Lord carried Judah and Jerusalem into captivity by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar. Thus the sacred line of high priests was not extinguished but preserved even in exile, to be restored under Zerubbabel and Jeshua (Ezra 2:2).

The sons of Levi are repeated — Gerson, Caath, and Merari — as the author now begins a fresh enumeration of the Levitical families in their broader scope, distinct from the strictly high-priestly succession already given.
The sons of Gerson are Libni and Shimei — these two form the two main sub-branches of the Gershonite family responsible for carrying the curtains and coverings of the Tabernacle.
The sons of Caath are Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel — the Caathites bore the most sacred objects of the Tabernacle, including the Ark, the table, and the lampstand.

The sons of Merari are Mahli and Mushi. These are the three great family groupings of Levi according to their clans, each with distinct liturgical responsibilities assigned by Moses and later organized by David.
The descent of Gershom continues: Libni his son, Jahath his son, Zimmah his son — tracing the Gershonite line generation by generation through those who served in the sanctuary.
Joah his son, Iddo his son, Zerah his son, Jeatherai his son — completing several generations of the Gershonite sub-line descended from Libni.

The line of Caath resumes: Aminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son — Korah being the infamous leader of the rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Num. 16), though his sons did not perish with him (Num. 26:11) and remained a distinguished Levitical family.

On the sons of Aaron: Nadab and Abihu, who were slain by God for offering strange fire (Lev. 10:2). Only two survived — Eleazar and Ithamar. Eleazar, being the elder, succeeded Aaron in the high priesthood, and his descendants held it down to Ozi, who is mentioned shortly after.

After Ozi, the high priesthood passed to Eli, who was a descendant of Ithamar, not Eleazar. Four of Eli's descendants succeeded him, the last of whom was Abiathar, who was deprived of the high priesthood by Solomon on account of his conspiracy with Adonijah against Solomon. Sadoc, a descendant of Eleazar, was appointed in his place, and the high priesthood remained in his line until the Babylonian captivity. Therefore the four intermediate figures named here from Ozi to Sadoc — namely Zaraias, Meraioth, Amarias, and Achitob — were not high priests; but those after Sadoc were.
The Azariah mentioned here was that courageous High Priest who resisted King Uzziah (also called Azariah) when the king sought to offer incense, and rebuked him for it.

These are the ones whom David appointed over the singers of the house of the Lord, from the time the Ark was placed there. They ministered before the tabernacle of the testimony with song until Solomon built the house of the Lord in Jerusalem, standing in their appointed order of ministry.

Heman the singer, son of Joel, son of Samuel, is listed here as the chief among those who stood with their sons from the sons of Kohath. His genealogy is traced back through Elkanah, Jeroham, Eliel, Tohu, Zuph, and further ancestors, all the way to Levi son of Israel.

Asaph, the brother of Heman, stood at his right hand. His genealogy is traced: Asaph son of Berachiah, son of Shimea, son of Michael, son of Baaseiah, son of Malchijah, and further ancestors through Gershom son of Levi.
The sons of Merari, the brothers of the others, stood on the left. Their chief was Ethan son of Kishi, son of Abdi, son of Malluch, and further ancestors through Mushi, son of Merari, son of Levi.

Aaron and his sons burned incense on the altar of burnt offering and on the altar of incense, and were responsible for all the work of the Holy of Holies, and for making intercession for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded.
These were the dwelling places of Aaron's sons, assigned by lot to the families of the Kohathites. They received Hebron in the land of Judah with its surrounding pasturelands.

The fields and villages of the city of Hebron itself were given to Caleb son of Jephunneh — thus distinguishing the city and its suburbs (given to the priests) from its agricultural lands (given to Caleb).
To the sons of Aaron were given the cities of refuge: Hebron, Libnah with its pasturelands, Jattir, Eshtemoa, Holon, Debir, Ashan, Beth-shemesh — all with their surrounding lands.
From the tribe of Benjamin: Geba, Alemeth, and Anathoth with their pasturelands — thirteen cities in all according to their families.
To the remaining sons of Kohath, ten cities were given from the half-tribe of Manasseh as their possession.
To the sons of Gershom, thirteen cities were given according to their families from the tribes of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and from the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan.
To the sons of Merari, twelve cities were assigned by lot from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.
The children of Israel gave the Levites cities and their surrounding pasturelands, distributed by lot from the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin.
Among the cities of refuge given to the sons of Kohath were Shechem with its pasturelands in mount Ephraim, and Gezer with its pasturelands.
To the sons of Gershom from the half-tribe of Manasseh: Golan in Bashan with its pasturelands, and Ashtaroth with its pasturelands.
Beyond the Jordan, opposite Jericho toward the east, the sons of Merari received from the tribe of Reuben: Bezer in the wilderness, Jahzah, Kedemoth, and Mephaath, all with their pasturelands.
From the tribe of Gad: Ramoth in Gilead, Mahanaim, Heshbon, and Jazer, all with their surrounding pasturelands.

Korah (Core), whose rebellion against Moses caused him to be swallowed alive by the earth, is mentioned here because his sons were preserved unharmed by a miracle of God and are therefore enumerated in this register. Among these descendants was Elkanah, father of the Prophet Samuel, as is clear from verses 27, 34, and 37.
Samuel is named immediately after Elkanah (v. 27) in order to indicate that he was his son, as Vatablus observes.

Regarding "firstborn Vasseni and Abiah": 1 Samuel 8:2 gives the name of Samuel's firstborn as Joel, and the second as Abiah. Lapide resolves this by noting that Joel had two names, being also called Vasseni. Alternatively, the name Joel has dropped out of the text, and "Vasseni" (Hebrew וְהַשֵּׁנִי, meaning "and the second") simply introduces Abiah; the Septuagint renders it clearly: "And the sons of Samuel: his firstborn Joel and the second Abiah." These are the sons who, by their degenerate conduct, gave the people occasion to demand a king, leading Samuel to give them Saul (1 Sam. 8).
These are the dwelling places means: these are the priestly cities assigned to them by Joshua (ch. 21), where they are enumerated in nearly the same order as here.

Sons of Uzzi — 'Sons' here means 'son,' since only one is named, namely Izrahiah, because Ezra wished to trace only his lineage as the most distinguished. It is likely that Uzzi had several sons, but less notable ones. Certainly in these ten chapters many names of the descendants of the Patriarchs are passed over in silence, for it would have been lengthy and nearly endless to enumerate them all. Thus only the more illustrious are recorded here, the rest are omitted. So in Genesis 46:23 it is said "Sons of Dan, Hushim" — 'sons' meaning 'son,' for he had only one, from whom the entire tribe of Dan descended. This is an enallage of number, since the text enumerates all the Patriarchs' sons, of whom Dan had only one.
Sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, and Jediael, three. — These three were the most illustrious; for in total there were ten sons, who are enumerated in Genesis 46:21.

And his sister Molecheth bore Ishhod. — The Hebrew reads: 'And his sister Molecheth bore Ishhod.' So read the Septuagint, Pagninus, Vatablus, and others. But the Vulgate translated the proper names according to their meanings after its custom: for 'Molecheth' means 'queen,' and 'Ishhod' means 'man of glory' or 'man of beauty.' See what is said at vv. 22–23.

The men of Gath who were natives killed them (the sons of Ephraim, firstborn of Joseph), because they had come down to seize their possessions. — It is probable that Pharaoh had granted Joseph, whom he had set over Egypt to drive away the famine, certain possessions there, especially on the borders and in the land of Goshen which he had assigned to Joseph's brothers for the feeding of their flocks. Joseph, dying, left these possessions to his son Ephraim; Ephraim in turn handed them down to his sons, but the Gathites, neighbors to Egypt, killed them and seized their possessions — and this is what is recorded here. The Chaldean Paraphrast mentions this slaughter at Canticles 2:7, but after his custom mingles much that is fabulous with what is true.

And his daughter was Sheerah, who built Beth-horon. — 'Daughter' here means granddaughter or rather great-granddaughter; for a daughter of Ephraim living with him in Egypt could not have built Beth-horon and the two other cities that were in Canaan.

All Israel was numbered, and their sum was written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah; and they were carried away to Babylon because of their transgression. Lapide notes that this verse serves as a summary conclusion to the preceding genealogies, referencing the public registers of both kingdoms, and that the Babylonian captivity is explicitly attributed to the people's sin and defection from God.

Those who first dwelt in their possessions and cities were: Israel, the priests, the Levites, and the Nethinim. Lapide explains that these are the first returnees from Babylon who reoccupied their ancestral lands and cities, listing the four main classes: the lay Israelites, the priests, the Levites, and the Nethinim (temple servants).

The inhabitants of Jerusalem included children of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh. Lapide observes that while Jerusalem primarily belonged to Judah and Benjamin, members of the northern tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh also settled there after the return, showing the reunification of the people.

Uthai son of Ammihud, son of Omri, son of Imri, son of Bani, from the sons of Perez the son of Judah. Lapide notes the careful genealogical tracing back to Perez, one of the twin sons of Judah and Tamar, establishing the Judahite pedigree of the returnees.

Of the Shilonites: Asaiah the firstborn and his sons. Lapide identifies the Shilonites as descendants of Shelah, third son of Judah, and notes that Asaiah's priority as firstborn gave him precedence among this family group returning to Jerusalem.

Of the sons of Zerah: Jeuel and their brothers, six hundred and ninety. Lapide notes that Zerah was the twin brother of Perez, both born of Judah and Tamar, and that this considerable number of his descendants settled in Jerusalem after the return from captivity.

Of the sons of Benjamin: Sallu son of Meshullam, son of Hodaviah, son of Hasenuah. Lapide begins the enumeration of the Benjaminites who returned, noting that Benjamin's tribe, though small, was closely allied with Judah and maintained its identity through the captivity.

And Ibneiah son of Jeroham, and Elah son of Uzzi son of Michri, and Meshullam son of Shephatiah son of Reuel son of Ibnijah. Lapide notes the multiple Benjaminite family lines enumerated here, each traced through their paternal ancestors to establish legitimate claim to their place among the restored community.

Their brothers by their families numbered nine hundred and fifty-six. All these were heads of families in their fathers' houses. Lapide remarks on the significant number of Benjaminites returning, and emphasizes that these were not common people but chiefs and heads of clans, men of standing and authority.

Of the priests: Jedaiah, Joiarib, and Jachin. Lapide notes that these three priestly families were among the most distinguished of those returning, and that the priests are listed immediately after the lay leaders, reflecting their sacred dignity and importance in the restored community.

Azariah son of Hilkiah, son of Meshullam, son of Zadok, son of Meraioth, son of Ahitub, the ruler of the house of God. Lapide identifies this Azariah as the High Priest, tracing his lineage through the Zadokite line back to Ahitub, and notes the title 'ruler of the house of God' as designating the chief priestly office over the Temple.

Adaiah son of Jeroham, son of Pashhur, son of Malchijah; and Maasai son of Adiel, son of Jahzerah, son of Meshullam, son of Meshillemith, son of Immer. Lapide notes these represent two further priestly families of distinction among the returnees, both carefully identified through their ancestral lines to validate their priestly credentials.

Their brothers, heads of their families, one thousand seven hundred and sixty, very able men for the work of the service of the house of God. Lapide comments on the large number of priests returning — 1,760 — and emphasizes the phrase 'fortissimi robore' (very strong in ability), indicating they were chosen not only by lineage but by fitness and capability for the demanding liturgical service of the Temple.

Of the Levites: Shemaiah son of Hasshub, son of Azrikam, son of Hashabiah, of the sons of Merari. Lapide begins the enumeration of the Levites, noting that Merari was one of the three great Levitical clans (alongside Gershon and Kohath), and that Shemaiah is listed first among the Levites returning to Jerusalem.

Also Bakbakkar the carpenter, and Galal, and Mattaniah son of Micah, son of Zichri, son of Asaph. Lapide notes Asaph as the famous Levitical singer and psalmist appointed by David, and identifies his descendants among the returnees, suggesting the continuity of the Levitical musical tradition from David's time through the captivity and back.

And Obadiah son of Shemaiah, son of Galal, son of Jeduthun; and Berechiah son of Asa, son of Elkanah, who dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites. Lapide notes Jeduthun as another of David's three chief Levitical musicians, and remarks that Berechiah dwelt not in Jerusalem itself but in the surrounding villages of Netophah, serving the Temple from there.

The gatekeepers were: Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their brother Shallum the chief. Lapide explains that the gatekeepers (janitores) formed an important order of Levites whose duty was the custody of the Temple gates, and that Shallum held the position of chief among them, responsible for the oversight of all the others.

Until that time they had kept watch at the king's gate on the east, as gatekeepers of the camps of the sons of Levi by their divisions. Lapide notes that the eastern gate was the most important, as it faced the sunrise and was designated the royal gate, and that the Levitical gatekeepers served in rotating divisions going back to the time of the wilderness encampment.

Shallum son of Kore, son of Ebiasaph, son of Korah, with his brothers of his father's house, the Korahites, were over the work of the service, keepers of the thresholds of the tabernacle; and their families were over the entrance of the camp of the Lord, guarding the entrance. Lapide connects these Korahites to the famous Korah of the rebellion (Num. 16), noting that though Korah perished, his sons did not (Num. 26:11), and their descendants faithfully served as temple gatekeepers — a remarkable instance of divine mercy preserving a priestly line despite ancestral sin.

Phinehas son of Eleazar was formerly the ruler over them, and the Lord was with him. Lapide recalls Phinehas's celebrated zeal in slaying Zimri and Cozbi (Num. 25), for which God granted him and his descendants a covenant of everlasting priesthood, and notes that the Lord's presence with him was the source of the gatekeepers' dignity and effectiveness. # GEM

Zechariah son of Meshelemiah was the gatekeeper at the entrance of the tent of meeting. Lapide notes this Zechariah as a distinguished Levite appointed to the most sacred threshold, the entrance of the tabernacle of the testimony, a position of great honor requiring particular trustworthiness.

All these, chosen as gatekeepers at the thresholds, were two hundred and twelve. They were registered in their villages, whom David and Samuel the seer had appointed in their office of trust. Lapide draws attention to the joint appointment by David and Samuel, arguing this shows the antiquity and divine sanction of the office of gatekeeping, established by prophetic authority; he notes Samuel is called 'Videns' (the Seer), his ancient prophetic title. # GEM

Both they and their sons were in charge of the gates of the house of the Lord, namely the house of the tabernacle, by their divisions. Lapide emphasizes the hereditary nature of the office: the gatekeeping duty passed from fathers to sons, ensuring continuity of service and institutional memory in the care of God's house.

The gatekeepers were on the four sides: east, west, north, and south. Lapide notes the symbolic significance of the four-directional watch: the house of God was guarded on all sides, accessible from every quarter of the world, yet protected and ordered — an image, he suggests, of the universal Church guarded by its ministers.

Their brothers in their villages were to come in every seven days from time to time to serve with them. Lapide explains the rotational system: the principal gatekeepers resided in Jerusalem, while their brothers living in surrounding villages came on sabbaths and at appointed times to assist, ensuring the Temple was never without adequate guardianship.

For the four chief gatekeepers, who were Levites, were entrusted with the chambers and the treasuries of the house of God. Lapide notes that these four chief Levitical gatekeepers bore special responsibility not merely for the gates but for the storerooms and treasuries, requiring men of exceptional fidelity and proven integrity.

All Israel was numbered — this refers to the census taken by David in 2 Samuel 24. "And they were carried away to Babylon" — up to this point Ezra has reviewed the Hebrews who preceded the Babylonian captivity; henceforth he will enumerate those who returned from it and lived after it. Note that many from Israel, i.e., from the ten tribes, migrated to Jerusalem after the schism: partly because they abhorred the schism made by Jeroboam and wished to follow the ancient religion of the Jews and worship God in the temple; and partly because when the ten tribes were carried off into Assyria by Tiglath-Pileser and Shalmaneser, many fled to Jerusalem and joined themselves to the Jews. These were later carried off with the Jews to Babylon, and after 70 years returned with them to Jerusalem — and this is what is here described.

Who dwelt first in their possessions and cities — namely Israel (i.e., the Israelites), and the Priests, and the Levites, and the Nethinim, dwelling with the Jews in Judaea, thence carried with them to Babylon, and after the captivity was dissolved returning equally with them to Jerusalem and Judaea to their former possessions and cities, inhabiting them as they had done before.
There dwelt in Jerusalem of the children of Judah, and of the children of Benjamin, and of the children of Ephraim and Manasseh — Lapide then proceeds to enumerate these.

Uthai the son of Ammihud etc. — The Nethinim, that is, those given and offered to the temple, were the Gibeonites, who on account of the fraud committed were assigned to the temple by Joshua, condemned to carry water and wood to it. See what was said at Joshua 9:23 and 2 Esdras (Nehemiah) 11:1 and following, where the same are enumerated.

And of the Shilonites — understand: the sons. There was a notable man descended from the tribe of Judah, but not from Pharez or Zerah, as is clear from this passage, but descending from Shelah. For these three alone were the surviving sons of Judah, since Er and Onan were slain by God (Genesis 38).
In the villages — Hebrew: in the towns which were called Netophathite.

Until that time — that is, from the first institution of the temple gatekeepers until the destruction of the temple by the Chaldeans — "they kept watch," Hebrew: they stood guard, these Levites, by their courses; both at the other gates of the temple, and especially "at the King's Gate toward the east," that is, at the eastern gate of the temple, which was adjacent to the king's palace, through which the king was accustomed to enter the temple, and which was therefore called the King's Gate.

All these were chosen as gatekeepers... described in their own villages — For some Levites dwelt in Jerusalem, while others were distributed among other cities, towns, and villages. Hence verse 25 says: "They dwelt in their villages"; yet at set times when they were obliged to serve in rotation in the temple, they came to Jerusalem. These rotations and orders of service were established by David, and before him by Samuel the Seer or Prophet, and this "in his office" (in fide sua), that is, in the authority and power entrusted and committed to him by God and the commonwealth to do this. Secondly, "in his faithfulness" (Vatablus: on account of their faithfulness), because he knew them to be faithful and that they would faithfully discharge this duty. Thirdly, "by faithfulness" may be taken as a faithful, i.e., firm, stable, perpetual, and irrevocable office — as if to say David and Samuel established these gatekeepers in faithfulness, i.e., firmly and permanently, that they should exercise the office of gatekeeper faithfully, that is, steadily and perpetually. So Vatablus. For this is what the Hebrew בֶּאֱמוּנָתָם (beemunātham) signifies: "in faithfulness" or "in their stability."

Who dwelt in the chambers — that is, in the rooms and halls annexed to the temple — so that they might be free to sing the praises of God continually with hymns and psalms, and to instruct the people in the law of God. Hence these places were also called Synagogues.

The events of this chapter were treated in 1 Kings (1 Samuel) last chapter, where they were explained. The Philistines fought against Israel, and the men of Israel fled before them, falling wounded on Mount Gilboa.

Saul's arms were consecrated in the temple of Ashtaroth, as stated in 1 Kings (1 Samuel) last chapter. Ashtaroth was Venus, who was worshipped sometimes as a goddess, sometimes as a god. His head was affixed in the temple of Dagon, because the Philistines thus ascribed the victory to Dagon, and therefore offered the head of the slain King Saul as the most noble spoil to that idol.

He did not trust in the Lord — this was the primary cause of Saul's sin: namely, that distrusting God, whom he had offended through disobedience, he put his trust in the devil and summoned him through the witch of Endor (the Pythoness).
You shall feed my people Israel — that is, you shall rule them. For a king is the shepherd of peoples, as Homer says of Agamemnon.

Jesbaam son of Hachmoni, chief among the thirty, who lifted his spear against three hundred slain at one time. The prior portion of this chapter was treated at 2 Samuel 3:9 and throughout chapter 5; the latter portion at 2 Samuel 23:8 and following, where Lapide explains these matters.

He (Benaiah) struck down two ariel of Moab — that is, two lions of Moab, as it is called in 2 Samuel 23:20. These are called ariel, meaning "lion of God," that is, the greatest and most mighty. The Hebrews, Josephus, Lyra, the Glossa, and Cajetan hold that these two were the mightiest and most savage commanders of the Moabites, fierce as lions. But others take the word simply as lions, as it literally sounds. See what was said at 2 Samuel 23:20.

David placed him at his ear — that is, he made him his secretary, his intimate counselor and adviser, who would remind him of what needed to be done and suggest to him everything that was to be carried out; just as at Rome the Cardinals have their Auditors, as they are called. # GEM

On "brothers of Saul from Benjamin": these were kinsmen of Saul the Benjaminite. It was remarkable, almost a miracle, that God so honored David the exile and fugitive that many from the tribe of Benjamin and relatives of Saul preferred to follow David rather than Saul the reigning king who persecuted him. God was fighting for David against Saul, and David's innocence, modesty, and holiness drew all men to him, while Saul's pride, insolence, and wickedness drove them away. Hence three thousand Benjaminites preferred to follow David rather than Isbosheth son of Saul, as noted in verse 29.
On "the Korahites": as the Hebrews and the Septuagint read, these were the Korites, or descendants of Korah.
On "the Gadites": that is, from the tribe of Gad, or the Gadites.

These are they who crossed the Jordan in the first month (Nisan or the Passover month, corresponding partly to our March and partly to April), "when it was accustomed to overflow its banks, and they put all to flight": that is, these were the Gadites who with heroic spirit and valor were the first to dare to enter the Jordan divided by Joshua, and attacked the Canaanites standing on the opposite bank who were attempting to prevent the Hebrews from crossing, and drove them to flight.
Like the army of God: that is, like a very great army; for all things belonging to the greatest God are greatest.

Zadok also, a young man of outstanding character: this appears to be Zadok the High Priest, whom Saul appointed as High Priest after killing Ahimelech, who was of the line of Eli and Ithamar. He is called a youth because at that time he was younger; hence he later lived through the entire reign of David and served as High Priest jointly with Abiathar. Then when Solomon removed Abiathar, Zadok alone remained as High Priest, confirmed by Solomon. For although Jehoiada in the preceding verse is called a prince, he was not the High Priest but a prince, that is, a military commander who led the contingent of priests and Levites to David.

On "the sons of Issachar, learned men who knew all the seasons to direct what Israel ought to do, two hundred princes": Vatablus renders this as "princes skilled in recognizing opportunities," that is, knowing at what time and what action should be taken by seizing occasions — for example, that at this time, when Saul had been slain by the Philistines along with his entire army, David ought to be made king, as one accustomed to war and victorious, and therefore a terror to the Philistines capable of checking their pride and boldness from their recent victory, and that now the time had come when the promise of the kingdom made by God to David should be fulfilled. For there was no other who could come to the aid of Israel's affairs, now so ruinously fallen and afflicted, and restore them, except David. Therefore these princes persuaded not only their own tribe but also others to make David king. These men were therefore learned in the knowledge or understanding of times, that is, of occasions (as the Hebrews read), which is the practical knowledge most useful for human life, and in which consists true and perfect prudence — ethical, domestic, and political alike — as Solomon teaches in Ecclesiastes chapter 3: "All things have their season: a time to be born and a time to die; a time to kill and a time to heal; a time to weep and a time to laugh; a time to keep and a time to cast away; a time of war and a time of peace," etc. # GEM
All these warriors came to Hebron with a perfect heart to make David king over all Israel; and all the rest of Israel were likewise of one heart that David should be king.
They were there with David for three days eating and drinking, for their brothers had prepared for them.

Those nearby, as far as Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali, brought bread on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen for food: flour, fig cakes, raisins, wine, oil, oxen, and sheep in great abundance, for there was joy in Israel.

The sons of Issachar are discussed here. The Issacharites were farmers, and therefore they observed the changes of seasons, the conditions of air and winds, so that they might know when it was time for sowing, when for reaping, when for planting, when for sailing. This knowledge of times (scientia temporum) was necessary for farmers, as Virgil testifies in the Georgics. The Issacharites learned this knowledge of times partly through continuous observation and experience — namely from the prognostics of winds, stars, etc., continually observed — as Abulensis and Sanchez note; and partly they drew it from their Phoenician neighbors, namely the Tyrians and Sidonians (to whom the Issacharites brought their grain, wine, and oil, so that the Phoenicians might carry these goods by sea to the neighboring islands and sell them). For the Phoenicians, being dwellers by the sea, devoted themselves to navigation and commerce, and therefore observed the changes of the stars and seasons, so that they might know when, how, and where to sail.

David takes counsel with the tribunes, centurions, and all the princes regarding the Ark. The commentary notes that David proceeds not by his own authority alone but by common deliberation, showing the virtue of prudence in a king.

David addresses the whole assembly of Israel, saying: "If it pleases you, and if the word I speak proceeds from the Lord our God, let us send to our remaining brethren in all the regions of Israel, and to the priests and Levites dwelling in the suburbs of their cities, that they may gather to us." The commentary notes David's piety in seeking both human counsel and divine guidance.

The purpose declared: to bring back the Ark of God, which had not been sought in the days of Saul. This is a reproach against Saul, who neglected divine worship, whereas David makes restoration of the Ark his first concern upon establishing his kingdom.

The whole multitude agrees that it should be done, for the word pleased all the people. The commentary observes that holy undertakings, when proceeding from a devout leader, readily gain the assent of the faithful people.

David gathers all Israel from Shihor of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath, to bring the Ark of God from Kiriath-jearim. The commentary notes the vast extent of the assembly, signifying the unity of the whole people in this sacred act.

David and all Israel go up to the hill of Kiriath-jearim in Judah to bring the Ark of the Lord God who sits upon the cherubim, where His name is invoked. St. Jerome is cited regarding the significance of God being described as "sitting upon the cherubim," denoting His majesty and presence above the mercy seat.

The Ark is placed upon a new cart from the house of Abinadab, and Oza and his brother drive the cart. The commentary notes that a new cart was used as a sign of reverence, though later it is shown that this manner of transporting the Ark was contrary to the Levitical prescription, which required it to be carried on the shoulders of the Levites.

David and all Israel play before God with all their might, in songs, with harps, psalteries, and timbrels. The commentary praises this sacred joy as a model of liturgical worship, noting that bodily expressions of joy — singing, instruments, even dancing — are legitimate when directed purely to God's honor. # GEM
Hiram king of Tyre sends messengers to David, along with cedar timber and craftsmen of walls and wood, to build him a house. Lapide notes this chapter has been explained at 2 Kings 5.

David recognized that the Lord had confirmed him as king over Israel — this he inferred from seeing all the Israelites voluntarily and by common consent accepting him as king, subjecting themselves to him, and freely offering their help and service; and also from Hiram and neighboring kings honoring, loving, and fearing him as king of Israel.
David took additional wives in Jerusalem and begat sons and daughters.
These are the names of those born to him in Jerusalem: Shamua, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon.
Ibhar, Elishua, and Elpelet.
Nogah, Nepheg, and Japhia.
Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet.
When the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over all Israel, they all came up seeking him; and when David heard of it, he went out against them.
The Philistines came and spread themselves in the valley of Rephaim.
David consulted the Lord, saying: Shall I go up against the Philistines, and wilt thou deliver them into my hand? The Lord said: Go up, and I will deliver them into thy hand.
When they had gone up to Baal-perazim, David smote them there and said: God has divided my enemies by my hand, as waters are divided; therefore that place was called Baal-perazim.
The Philistines left their gods there, and David commanded that they be burned.
The Philistines again made a raid and spread themselves in the valley.

David again consulted God, and God said: Do not go up after them; circle around them and come against them opposite the pear trees. Lapide notes the Hebrew and patristic tradition (Jerome, Angelomus, and the Historia Scholastica) explain this strategy: David was not to advance directly but to encircle their camp, attacking from the rear, opposite the pear trees.
When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the pear trees, then go out to battle, for God has gone out before you to strike the camp of the Philistines.
David did as God commanded him, and he struck down the Philistine army from Gibeon to Gezer.
The fame of David spread through all regions, and the Lord brought the fear of him upon all nations.

David built houses for himself in the City of David, and prepared a place for the Ark of God, pitching a tabernacle for it. Lapide notes the order: David first established his own dwelling, then immediately turned his attention to providing a worthy place for the Ark of the Lord.

David declares it unlawful for anyone except the Levites to carry the Ark of God, since they were chosen by the Lord to carry it and to minister to Him forever. Lapide emphasizes that David here corrects his earlier error (when Uzzah was struck down) by insisting on strict observance of the Mosaic prescription that only Levites bear the Ark.

David assembles all Israel in Jerusalem so that the Ark of God might be brought to the place he had prepared for it. The universal gathering signifies the importance of the occasion and the communal nature of divine worship.
David also assembles the sons of Aaron and the Levites, carefully distinguishing the priests (sons of Aaron) from the Levites proper, both being necessary for the solemn translation of the Ark.
Of the sons of Kohath, Uriel was the chief, with 120 of his brethren. Lapide notes that the Kohathites had the most honorable duty of carrying the most sacred vessels, including the Ark itself.
Of the sons of Merari, Asaiah was the chief, with 220 of his brethren. These are enumerated to show the orderly arrangement David made for the sacred procession.
Of the sons of Gershom, Joel was the chief, with 130 of his brethren. The three great Levitical families — Kohath, Merari, and Gershom — are all represented.
Of the sons of Elizaphan, Shemaiah was the chief, with 200 of his brethren. Elizaphan was a son of Uzziel, son of Kohath, showing the subdivision of Levitical families.
Of the sons of Hebron, Eliel was the chief, with 80 of his brethren. Hebron here denotes a Levitical family descended from Kohath, not the city.
Of the sons of Uzziel, Amminadab was the chief, with 112 of his brethren. All six Levitical families are now enumerated, showing David's thoroughness in organizing the sacred ministry.

David called the priests Zadok and Abiathar, and the Levitical chiefs Uriel, Asaiah, Joel, Shemaiah, Eliel, and Amminadab. Lapide notes that David summons both the priestly and the Levitical orders together, showing the proper hierarchy in divine service.

David commands them: as princes of the Levitical families, they must sanctify themselves together with their brethren and bring the Ark of the Lord God of Israel to the place prepared for it. Sanctification here involves ritual purification, continence, and interior devotion as preparation for handling sacred things.

David explains why such care is needed: because at the beginning, when they were not present (or did not follow proper procedure), the Lord struck them — referring to the death of Uzzah. Lapide stresses this as a warning that irreverence toward sacred things, even from ignorance or negligence, draws divine punishment.

The priests and Levites sanctified themselves to carry the Ark of the Lord God of Israel. Lapide notes that this obedient sanctification contrasts with the earlier negligence, showing that the proper disposition of ministers is essential for sacred functions.

The sons of Levi carried the Ark of God on their shoulders with poles, as Moses had commanded according to the word of the Lord (cf. Num. 7:9; Exod. 25:14). Lapide emphasizes that strict adherence to the divinely prescribed manner of transporting the Ark was the lesson learned from Uzzah's death.

David instructs the Levitical princes to appoint singers with musical instruments — psalteries, harps, and cymbals — so that joyful sound might resound on high. Lapide sees in this the Church's use of sacred music in divine worship, which lifts the mind to God.

The Levites appointed Heman son of Joel, Asaph son of Berechiah from his brethren, and from the sons of Merari, Ethan son of Kushaiah. These three were the chief musicians, representing the three Levitical clans, each providing a principal cantor for the sacred procession.

With them in the second rank were: Zechariah, Ben, Jaaziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, Obed-edom, and Jeiel — who were doorkeepers. Lapide notes the careful ordering of ministers in ranks, reflecting the hierarchical organization of divine worship.
The singers Heman, Asaph, and Ethan were appointed to sound the bronze cymbals. These three chief cantors led the musical praise, each representing one of the great Levitical families.

Zechariah, Uzziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Maaseiah, and Benaiah sang with psalteries set to Alamoth (i.e., in the soprano or higher register). Lapide notes the careful assignment of different instruments and vocal registers to different groups of Levites.

Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, Obed-edom, Jeiel, and Azaziah played harps for the octave, singing the victory hymn (epinicion). Lapide explains "pro octava" as referring to the eighth mode or octave register, possibly the bass, providing a deeper musical foundation to the sacred chant.

Chenaniah, prince of the Levites in prophecy (i.e., sacred song), presided over the chanting of the melody, for he was very skilled. Lapide interprets "prophetia" here as sacred song or psalmody, since the ancient prophets often prophesied accompanied by music (cf. 1 Sam. 10:5), and notes that Chenaniah's wisdom qualified him as director of the sacred choir. # GEM
Berechiah and Elkanah were doorkeepers of the Ark. Their role was to guard the Ark against any unauthorized approach, recalling the lesson of Uzzah.

The priests Shebaniah, Jehoshaphat, Nethanel, Amasai, Zechariah, Benaiah, and Eliezer sounded trumpets before the Ark of God; and Obed-edom and Jehiah were also doorkeepers of the Ark. Lapide notes the distinct roles: priests blow the silver trumpets (as prescribed in Num. 10:8) while the Levites play other instruments, preserving proper hierarchical order.

David, all the elders of Israel, and the commanders of thousands went to bring up the Ark of the covenant of the Lord from the house of Obed-edom with joy. The participation of the civil and military leadership alongside the religious ministers shows that the translation of the Ark was a national act of worship.

When God had helped the Levites who carried the Ark of the covenant of the Lord, they sacrificed seven bulls and seven rams. Lapide notes that God's assistance was manifested in the safe and unhindered carrying of the Ark, prompting immediate thanksgiving through sacrifice — seven being the number of perfection, signifying a perfect holocaust of praise.

David was clothed in a robe of fine linen, as were all the Levites who carried the Ark, the singers, and Chenaniah the director of the singers; David also wore a linen ephod. Lapide notes that while the linen robe was common to all the ministers, the ephod was a priestly garment that David wore as king-priest in the theocratic sense, not arrogating the priesthood but expressing his unique royal-sacral dignity before the Lord.

All Israel brought up the Ark of the covenant of the Lord with shouting, with the sound of the horn, with trumpets, with cymbals, with psalteries, and with harps. Lapide sees in this joyful, multi-instrumental procession a type of the Church's solemn liturgical celebrations, in which all the senses and all the faithful unite in glorifying God.

Some conclude from this that Oza was struck by God because he, not being a priest, touched the ark; for it was the priests' duty to touch and carry it on their shoulders. See the commentary on 2 Samuel 6. Others infer from the phrase "sanctify yourselves" that Oza was struck because he had contracted some defilement and had not purified and sanctified himself before touching the ark. But "sanctify yourselves" pertains to the priests, who could not touch the ark unless they had first purged themselves from every legal uncleanness; otherwise they were to be struck by God just as Oza was — and this is all David means here.

They sang on the nablia hidden or secret things — namely, Psalms inscribed in Hebrew "Alamoth," meaning secret and hidden things, such as Psalms 9 and 46. These two psalms are so fitting to this passage that they appear to have been composed by David on the occasion of this translation of the ark. The nablum was a musical instrument which received air in a chamber like a bladder (for "nebel" in Hebrew means a skin or wineskin) and emitted it through a pipe in harmony, without anyone blowing into it. Hence the one who used it was called a bagpiper or "Ascaules," for "askos" in Greek means a wineskin.

They sang on the cithara for the octave an epinicion — that is, they sang psalms inscribed in Hebrew "al-hasheminith," meaning "upon the eighth" or "for the octave," either because they were to be sung in eight voices or on an eight-stringed instrument (for some instruments were tetrachords, others heptachords, others decachords). For "sheminith" means eight; see the commentary on 2 Samuel 6:13.
