For "epinicion," the Hebrew is "netsach," which Vatablus translates "to prevail," that is, to raise the voice so as to surpass other singers, as the Alto or Treble does now; for the Alto by elevation of voice surpasses the Tenor, Counter-tenor, and Bass. Better, the Vulgate translates it "epinicion," that is, a triumphal song or song of victory. See 2 Samuel 6:13.

Chenaniah, prince of the Levites, led the prophecy for the singing of the melody. For "prophecy" the Hebrew has "massa," meaning elevation or lifting up, hence a burden taken up upon the shoulders, and metaphorically a heavy and burdensome prophecy. First, some translate: Chenaniah presided over the burden and those who bore it, because it fell to him to instruct the ark-bearers how they ought to proceed, rest, change positions, etc. Second, others translate: He presided over the prophecy, that is, he prescribed to the singers the prophecies they were to sing; for singers are sometimes called prophets, and to prophesy is the same as to sing and praise God — so Saul "prophesied," that is, sang the praises of God (1 Samuel 10:5). Third, the Hebrews according to St. Jerome in his Traditions say he presided over the prophecy in this sense: he recalled to the singers' memory the melodies and words of the prophecies. Fourth and best, the Septuagint translates: He was prince in the elevation of voice — that is, Chenaniah was precentor, intoning with a higher voice and leading the melody of the psalm to be sung, which the other singers then followed, as cantors and choir directors do in the choir. That this is so is clear from the Vulgate's explanatory phrase "for leading the melody," where the Hebrew uses the same word "massa" which the Vulgate had just translated "prophecy" — so "prophecy" here was nothing other than "melody." "He was very wise" — the Hebrew is "mebin," meaning intelligent, that is, most skilled in music and singing; for such is what is fitting for precentors.

When God helped the Levites — so that they would feel no burden in carrying the ark, say the Rabbis; but this is a fable. Hear Lyranus from the Hebrews, aptly applied to the present matter: "The Hebrews say," he writes, "that this help is understood through the fact that the ark carried itself — that is, that by divine power it was carried in such a way that the Levite bearers felt no weight at all; and for that miracle the sacrifice was made." But I say: God helped them by animating them, by supplying strength and especially love, so that they willingly bore the ark and thus scarcely felt the burden. Therefore that miracle was not so much one of power as of charity; for love made that burden, otherwise very heavy, seem most light to those who loved. Josephus adds that those who went before the ark singing, dancing, and playing musical instruments, though they spent the whole day in that labor, felt no weariness from their toil; for the love with which they served the divine oracle did not allow their strength to diminish but rather made them more vigorous with each passing hour.
David was clothed in a robe of byssus — that is, in a linen ephod, which elsewhere is called byssine; for byssus is a finer and more delicate linen.

The ark of God was brought in and placed in the middle of the tabernacle that David had pitched for it, and burnt offerings and peace offerings were offered before God. David had prepared a special tabernacle on Mount Zion distinct from the ancient tabernacle at Gibeon, and here the ark was solemnly installed with sacrifices of both holocausts (total offerings to God) and peace offerings (shared between God and the people).

When David had finished offering the holocausts and peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of the Lord. As king and in some sense a priestly figure, David performed this blessing, acting in the name of God toward the assembled congregation, just as priests were appointed to bless Israel.

David distributed to every Israelite, man and woman alike, a loaf of bread, a portion of roasted meat, and a cake made with oil. This generous distribution of food to the entire people — without distinction of sex or status — reflects David's royal munificence and his desire that all Israel share in the joy of this sacred occasion.

David appointed certain Levites to minister before the ark of the Lord, to commemorate, to thank, and to praise the Lord God of Israel. Their ministry was threefold: to record and proclaim God's deeds (recordarentur), to glorify Him (glorificarent), and to praise Him (laudarent) — a perpetual liturgical office instituted by David.

Asaph was appointed as chief, with Zechariah second; then Jaiel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Mattithiah, Eliab, Benaiah, and Obed-edom. Jehiel was assigned the instruments of the psaltery and lyres; Asaph was to sound the cymbals. Each Levite had a distinct role in the sacred music, showing that ordered worship requires assigned duties and specific gifts.

Benaiah and Jahaziel the priests were appointed to blow the trumpets continually before the ark of the covenant of the Lord. The priests sounded the trumpets while the Levites played the other instruments, maintaining the distinction of orders even in liturgical music.

On that day David first appointed Asaph and his brethren to give thanks to the Lord. This marks the formal institution of the Levitical choir as a permanent liturgical office — a key moment in the history of Israelite worship that David organized with great care.

Give thanks to the Lord, call upon His name, make known His deeds among the peoples. This canticle, which follows (vv. 8–36), is compiled largely from Psalms 105, 96, and 106. The opening verse calls for three acts: gratitude, invocation of the divine name, and missionary proclamation of God's works among the nations.

Sing to Him, sing praise to Him, and tell of all His wonders. The triple command — sing, sing psalms, narrate — corresponds to different modes of praising God: vocal song, instrumental psalmody, and preaching of His marvelous works. Praise of God is thus both interior and exterior, musical and verbal.

Glory in His holy name; let the heart of those who seek the Lord rejoice. The name of God is called holy because it is the expression of His nature and holiness. Those who seek God (quaerentes) are distinguished from the merely curious: they seek Him with desire, love, and perseverance, and their hearts are filled with true joy.

Seek the Lord and His strength; seek His face always. God's strength (virtutem ejus) refers to His power manifested in acts of salvation, or perhaps to the ark itself as the symbol of His might. To seek His face is to seek His presence, favor, and vision — the ultimate goal of the spiritual life. # GEM

Remember the wonders He has done, His signs and the judgments of His mouth. The faithful are called to memorial — an active, grateful remembrance of God's mighty acts — not merely as historical recall but as a living faith that draws present strength from past deeds of God.

O seed of Israel His servant, children of Jacob, His chosen ones. Israel is reminded of its election: it is both the seed of the servant Abraham/Israel and the chosen (electi) of God. Election carries both privilege and obligation — to serve and to praise.

He is the Lord our God; His judgments are in all the earth. Though God is Israel's God in a special covenantal sense, His dominion and judicial governance extend over the whole earth and all peoples, for He is the universal Creator and Lord.

Remember His covenant forever, the word He commanded for a thousand generations. The covenant (pactum) is not a temporary arrangement but an everlasting one, binding across countless generations. The "thousand generations" is a Hebraic expression for perpetuity, underscoring the absolute fidelity of God to His promises.

The covenant He made with Abraham, and His oath to Isaac. The Abrahamic covenant is here recalled as the foundation of Israel's relationship with God — sealed by divine oath, making it absolutely inviolable, since God cannot lie.

He confirmed it to Jacob as a statute and to Israel as an everlasting covenant. What was promised to Abraham and sworn to Isaac was formally established (constituit) as a binding law for Jacob/Israel, showing the progressive confirmation of the covenant through the patriarchal line.

Saying: To you I will give the land of Canaan as the portion of your inheritance. The land of Canaan is given as funiculum — literally a measuring line or cord — referring to the allotted portion measured out by lot or line, as in the division of the Promised Land among the tribes.

When they were few in number, very few, and strangers in it. This recalls the patriarchal period when Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob were wandering sojourners (coloni — tenant farmers or resident aliens) in Canaan, with no legal claim or settled territory of their own.

And they wandered from nation to nation, and from one kingdom to another people. The patriarchs moved repeatedly: from Chaldea to Canaan, from Canaan to Egypt, from region to region within Canaan itself — always as foreigners dependent on God's protection.

He allowed no one to oppress them; He rebuked kings for their sake. God protected the wandering patriarchs against powerful rulers — as when He rebuked Pharaoh for Sarah's sake (Gen. 12) and Abimelech for Rebekah's sake (Gen. 20) — demonstrating His sovereign care over His elect even when they were powerless.

Touch not my anointed ones, and do my prophets no harm. The patriarchs are called "anointed" (christos) not because they received literal anointing but because they were consecrated by divine election and the Holy Spirit. They are also called prophets because they received divine revelations and proclaimed God's will. Augustine and others note that this verse protects not only priests and kings but all who are consecrated to God. # GEM

Sing to the Lord, all the earth; proclaim His salvation from day to day. The canticle now universalizes its scope: all the earth (omnis terra) is summoned to praise. "Salvation" (salutare) refers ultimately to the salvation wrought by Christ, of whom all Israel's deliverances were figures — hence the daily proclamation.

Declare His glory among the nations, His wonders among all peoples. The mission of Israel — and later of the Church — is to be the herald of God's glory to all nations. The marvelous works (mirabilia) of God are the subject of this evangelizing proclamation.

For great is the Lord and greatly to be praised; He is to be feared above all gods. The incomparable greatness of the Lord is the basis for His universal praise and worship. He is "terrible" (horribilis) above all the so-called gods — inspiring reverential awe that the false gods, being mere idols, can never evoke.

For all the gods of the peoples are idols, but the Lord made the heavens. The contrast is absolute: the pagan deities are mere idols (idola — vanities, nothings), while the Lord is the Creator of the heavens themselves. This is a fundamental argument for monotheism: the Maker of all things cannot be compared to things made by human hands.

Splendor and majesty are before Him; strength and joy are in His place. In God's presence (coram eo) are glory and magnificence; in His dwelling (loco ejus — the sanctuary, heaven) are power and gladness. Joy is thus a characteristic of the divine dwelling, and those who dwell with God share in this divine gladness.

Ascribe to the Lord, O families of peoples, ascribe to the Lord glory and strength. All the families (familiae) of the nations — not just Israel — are summoned to render to God the glory and dominion that belong to Him alone. This is a call to universal worship.

Give to the Lord the glory due His name; bring an offering and come before Him; worship the Lord in the beauty of holiness. The "beauty of holiness" (decor sanctus) refers to the dignity and reverence with which God is to be worshipped — with pure hearts, sacred vestments, and holy rites. The Fathers apply this to the beauty of virtuous souls who appear before God.

Tremble before Him, all the earth; the world is firmly established and cannot be moved. God's sovereignty over creation is absolute: the earth trembles at His presence, yet it stands firm because He has established it on an immovable foundation. The stability of the world reflects the constancy of its Creator.

Let the heavens be glad and the earth rejoice, and let them say among the nations: The Lord reigns. All creation — heavens and earth — is called to eschatological joy at God's universal kingship. The proclamation "The Lord reigns" anticipates the full manifestation of His kingdom, which the Fathers see as fulfilled in Christ's reign through the Church and ultimately in glory.

Let the sea roar and all that fills it; let the fields exult and everything in them. Even inanimate creation — sea and field — is poetically summoned to praise. This use of personification (prosopopoeia) expresses that the entire created order exists to glorify God and participates, in its own manner, in the cosmic hymn of praise.

Then shall the trees of the forest sing for joy before the Lord, for He comes to judge the earth. The coming of the Lord to judge is presented not as a threat but as a cause for universal rejoicing: the just governance of God brings order and restoration to all creation. The Fathers see here a prophecy of the Last Judgment, which the righteous welcome with joy. # GEM

Give thanks to the Lord, for He is good; for His mercy endures forever. This doxological conclusion — recurring throughout the Psalms — summarizes the entire basis for praise: God's goodness (bonitas) and His everlasting mercy (misericordia in aeternum). Lapide notes that this verse was sung antiphonally and became the great acclamation of Israel's liturgy, prefiguring the Church's perpetual Eucharist (thanksgiving).

David commanded Asaph and his companions to sing this hymn of thanksgiving to God because he had transferred the ark (and in it God dwelling) into his citadel of Zion, so that he might have God always present, protecting, directing, and strengthening him. This hymn is composed from Psalm 104 up to verse 23, and thereafter from Psalm 95. It is recorded here somewhat differently than in those psalms because David appears to have first dictated it here in rough form, then later polished and refined it in the Psalter. It is a psalm and belongs to the Psalter; therefore it will be explained there in its proper place. Here only brief notes are made on where it differs from the cited psalms.

Seek the Lord and His strength (virtus): the Translator reads 'azzo, meaning His strength or power — that is, the ark in which God displayed His power by defeating Jericho and the Canaanites. David invites all to come to the ark and adore God before it. The Septuagint, reading with different vowel points 'azu in the Kal imperative, translates in Psalm 104:5 as "be strengthened," meaning God will confirm and strengthen you so that no enemy, no adversity, no temptation can prevail against you.
He suffered no man to do them wrong: Vatablus notes the Hebrew 'azar means both to calumniate and to do violence. Hence the Septuagint at Psalm 104:15 translates: "He suffered no man to harm them."

Confession (Septuagint: praise) and magnificence before Him; strength and joy in His place: Vatablus renders this as "Festivity and magnificence are before Him, power and gladness in His place." But Psalm 95:6 in the Septuagint has: "Confession and beauty in His sight; holiness and magnificence in His sanctuary" — that is, in the ark and the tabernacle of the ark erected by David.

Bring to the Lord glory and dominion — Hebrew: "and strength." The sense of these two verses is: Give to God in this tabernacle before the ark confession, that is, praise and magnificence, that is, a magnificent proclamation of His magnificent works which magnifies God and shows Him to be supremely great; likewise strength and joy, so that you celebrate His mighty deeds by which He fought for Israel and thus gave us matter for rejoicing and triumph.

Adore the Lord in holy comeliness — Hebrew: "in the comeliness of holiness," that is, of the holy ark. Vatablus: "in the splendid and magnificent sanctuary." But Psalm 95:9 says: "Adore the Lord in His holy court."

Let the sea roar and its fullness: Vatablus renders this as "Let the sea thunder, and all that is in it — all fish, gems, pearls, etc." — for the waves of the sea in a storm, crashing against one another, produce a roar like thunder. But Psalm 95:12 has: "Let the sea be moved (tremble before God's majesty and omnipotence) and its fullness." Again, the sea thunders when it sends up vapors on high which, enclosed and compressed in clouds, as they burst forth by force, stir up thunder and lightning. Let the sea thunder therefore to display the power of God its Creator and to strike the impious with fear of Him.
David left Asaph and his brothers there before the ark of the covenant of the Lord to minister in the presence of the ark continually, day by day, each according to his appointed service.
Obed-edom and his brothers, sixty-eight in number, along with Obed-edom son of Jeduthun and Hosa, were appointed as gatekeepers.

Zadok the priest (high priest) and his brothers the priests (minor priests) served before the tabernacle of the Lord in the high place at Gibeon. Zadok had exercised the high priesthood before Saul after Saul had killed Ahimelech the High Priest, at which time Ahimelech's son Abiathar fled to David. Thus the High Priest under Saul was Zadok; under David it was Abiathar. After Saul's death, David did not wish to remove Zadok from the high priesthood, but divided it between them so that Abiathar exercised the pontificate in Jerusalem where the ark was, while Zadok served at Gibeon where the tabernacle made by Moses stood.
They offered burnt offerings to the Lord upon the altar of holocaust continually, morning and evening, according to all that is written in the law of the Lord which He commanded Israel.
After him Heman and Jeduthun and the rest of those chosen, each designated by name, gave thanks to the Lord, for His mercy endures forever.
Heman and Jeduthun played trumpets and cymbals and all instruments of music for singing to God; and the sons of Jeduthun were made gatekeepers.
All the people returned to their homes, and David returned to bless his own house as well.
He shaved them (Heb. *legallech*) means he shaved half the hair of the head and beard, as is stated in 2 Kings 10:4.

They hired thirty-two thousand chariots means charioteers who fought from scythed chariots. This is a metonymy: the container is put for the contained, i.e., the chariot for the chariot-soldier. In ancient times, individual soldiers of greater power each had their own chariot, just as cavalry soldiers have their own horse from which they fight, as is clear from verse 10.
David killed seven thousand chariots of the Syrians means soldiers who fought from chariots, or certainly the horses drawing the chariots — for chariots themselves can be burned, but not killed.

After the year's cycle, at the time when kings customarily go out to war, Joab gathered the army and the strength of the military force, and laid waste the land of the Ammonites; he then marched and besieged Rabbah. Meanwhile David remained in Jerusalem while Joab struck and destroyed Rabbah. Lapide notes the parallel with 2 Samuel 11:1, observing that this is the very occasion on which David's sin with Bathsheba occurred, though the Chronicler omits that shameful episode entirely, as his purpose is to record David's glorious deeds rather than his failings.

David took the crown of Milcom (or Melchom) from his head — that is, the crown of the idol of the Ammonites, as some interpret it, or the crown of the Ammonite king — and found in it gold weighing a talent, and most precious gems, and he made from it a diadem for himself. He also carried off very great plunder from the city. Lapide notes the enormous weight of a talent of gold, signifying the extraordinary richness of this crown, and draws attention to the variant reading "Milcom," the idol of the Ammonites, suggesting David stripped the very god of his enemies of his crown as a trophy of divine victory.

David brought out the people who were in the city, and made tribulas (threshing sledges), beams, and iron-fitted wagons pass over them, so that they were cut apart and crushed. Thus David did to all the cities of the Ammonites, and then returned with all his people to Jerusalem. Lapide discusses the severity of this punishment, noting the cruelty of death by threshing instruments, and compares the parallel in 2 Samuel 12:31, defending David's action as just punishment for a people who had gravely insulted his ambassadors and made persistent war against Israel.

After these things war was joined at Gezer against the Philistines, in which Sibbechai the Hushathite struck down Sippai, who was of the race of the Raphaim, and humbled them. Lapide identifies Sibbechai as one of David's mighty warriors, and notes that the Raphaim were a race of giants descended from Rapha, whose remnants persisted among the Philistines; the defeat here signals the continued diminishment of this formidable lineage.

Another war was also waged against the Philistines, in which Adeodatus (Elhanan) son of Saltus (Jair) the Bethlehemite struck down the brother of Goliath the Gittite, whose spear's shaft was like a weaver's beam. Lapide carefully resolves the apparent contradiction with 2 Samuel 21:19, which seems to say Elhanan slew Goliath himself, explaining that the Chronicler's text clarifies it was Goliath's brother, thus defending the inerrancy of Scripture and harmonizing the two accounts. He notes that "fratrem Goliath" is the correct reading.

But another battle occurred at Gath, in which there was a very tall man who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all, and he too was born of the race of Rapha. Lapide marvels at this as a prodigy of nature, citing it as evidence that the giant races of Canaan, though diminished, still produced monstrous individuals; he notes that supernumerary digits were sometimes regarded in antiquity as a sign of extraordinary bodily vigor.

This man blasphemed Israel, and Jonathan the son of Shimea, brother of David, struck him down. These are the sons of Rapha in Gath, who fell by the hand of David and his servants. Lapide concludes by noting that all these giants — the remnants of the Raphaim — were destroyed through David and his men, seeing in this a figure of how Christ through His members overthrows the spiritual giants, that is, the demons and great enemies of the Church. He emphasizes that the blasphemy of Israel provoked divine justice, and that Jonathan's valor reflects the grace God bestows on those who defend His people's honor. # GEM

And Satan rose up against Israel and moved David to number Israel. The Hebrew reads *Malcham* (their king), referring to the idol of the Ammonites. The Septuagint agrees: the crown of idols such as Melchom was to be burned along with the idols themselves, as commanded in Deuteronomy 7; therefore David could not lawfully fashion a royal crown for himself from it.

Satan rose up against Israel and incited David to number the people. This is the synopsis of the chapter: David, by numbering the people, provokes God to anger, and from three punishments offered by God he chooses the last; which being sent destroys many, but at length David, appeasing God by prayer and sacrifice, brings it to an end.

David said to Joab and to the princes of the people: "Go and number Israel from Beersheba to Dan, and bring me the count that I may know it." The command extends the census across the full breadth of the land.

Joab answered: "May the Lord increase his people a hundredfold beyond what they are; are not all, my lord the king, your servants? Why does my lord seek this, which would be reckoned as sin against Israel?" Joab here shows more wisdom and piety than David himself, recognizing that the census was displeasing to God and would be imputed as sin to the nation.
Nevertheless the king's word prevailed over Joab, and Joab went out and traveled throughout all Israel and returned to Jerusalem. The persistence of David's command overcame Joab's godly resistance.
Joab gave David the number of those he had counted: all Israel was one million one hundred thousand men who drew the sword; of Judah alone, four hundred and seventy thousand warriors.

Levi and Benjamin he did not number, because the king's command was abominable to Joab. The tribe of Levi was exempt as sacred to God, and Benjamin was perhaps spared out of reverence or because Joab's repugnance at the sinful command caused him to leave the census incomplete.

Or three years of famine: In 2 Samuel 24:13, it says "seven years of famine." The explanation is that God's first offer to David was for seven years, but when David entreated that so long a punishment be mitigated, God moderated the penalty and reduced it to three years. Thus in Genesis 18, God descended from fifty just men down to ten, whom if He had found in Sodom He would have spared; and similarly to Ezekiel (ch. 4), when he entreated concerning human dung for food, God granted him bovine dung instead.
Ornan: In 2 Samuel 24:23, his name is given with a slight variation as Araunah, and he was a man of rank — that is, a petty king or prince among the Jebusites.
This is the house of God — that is, on this site, namely the threshing floor of Ornan on Mount Moriah, God commands the Temple to be built.
Solomon is a young child — meaning that Solomon, in relation to so great and magnificent a building project, is as it were a small boy; though in fact he was already about twenty years old.

One hundred thousand talents of gold — this equals twelve and a half million pounds of gold, amounting to one thousand two hundred million French gold coins. Likewise he left the same in silver, namely one thousand thousand talents of silver, equaling another twelve hundred million French gold coins; for anciently one pound of gold was worth ten pounds of silver. In total, therefore, he left two thousand four hundred million gold coins. This immense and almost incredible sum of gold and silver is explained by the fact that nothing is impossible or difficult for God, who easily supplied David with all this abundance for so magnificent a Temple. Hence when God wished to see the Temple restored after its burning by the Chaldeans through Zerubbabel and the Jews, He encouraged the poor people for the work saying: "The silver is mine and the gold is mine" (Haggai 2:9). So too St. Francis of Assisi and St. Francis of Paola — founders respectively of the Friars Minor and the Minims — both poor men commanded by God to build churches, built them, with God providing the costs through the alms of the pious. How much more, if called by God to build the mystical temple of perfection in our own souls or those of our neighbors, should we cheerfully do so trusting in His help. For God will be present and give strength and courage; for "the earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof," and as St. Leo says: "He justly presses with the command who anticipates with assistance." # GEM

Vilalpandus (in his commentary on Ezekiel, vol. 2) argues over many chapters that David collected such vast wealth partly from revenues, partly from Ophir, partly from spoils and tribute of enemies, and partly from the annual census of Israel by which each person paid half a shekel yearly to the Temple (Exod. 30:13). Even Israelites dispersed throughout the world sent this head-tax annually to Jerusalem, and since their number was enormous, they accumulated a vast sum of gold. Vilalpandus adds that David and Solomon in annual revenues and wealth surpassed the tribute and riches of the Romans, Chaldeans, Persians, and all kings and monarchs.

If one asks how David says in this verse that he collected all this "in his poverty," the answer is threefold: first, because all these things were small and few relative to God's dignity and majesty; second, "in poverty" means in great labor and affliction; third and chiefly, because as the context implies, all these things belonged to God, and therefore were not David's own, for he was poor of himself and had nothing except what he had received from God.

Ad universa impendia — The Hebrew and Chaldean have "and besides these add more"; the Septuagint: "and add to these"; Vatablus: "to which you may add more." Hence Solomon and the people added many donations to what David had left, to furnish and adorn more magnificently so magnificent a building.
The sons of Aaron had these divisions: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar were the sons of Aaron.
Nadab and Abihu died before their father without children, so Eleazar and Ithamar exercised the priestly office.

David divided them — that is, through Zadok from the sons of Eleazar, and Ahimelech from the sons of Ithamar — according to their courses. This means David divided all priests, descendants of Eleazar and Ithamar, into 24 classes. David did this not personally, but through the two high priests: Zadok, who was from the line of Eleazar, and Ahimelech, who was from the line of Ithamar. For this distribution of priests pertained to the High Priest, not to the king.

David divided both families among themselves by lot, so that no one could complain of being unjustly passed over, or another advanced by favoritism, but all would be reckoned of equal right and condition. Therefore the matter was settled by lot, and the lot assigned to each his own order and turn. "For they were princes of the sanctuary" — there were 24 priestly families, and each had its own chief, namely the firstborn, who presided over all in his turn and directed all things in the temple. He was therefore the chief priest of his own class, and for this reason was called Pontifex (High Priest). There were thus 24 high priests or princes of the priests, of whom frequent mention is made in the Gospels; but above all of these the supreme High Priest, namely Zadok, was eminent and presided.

Ahimelech the son of Abiathar — from this it appears that Abiathar the High Priest, being ill or occupied with other matters, delegated his duties to his son Ahimelech, so that he might assist with Zadok the High Priest and distribute these 24 priestly classes.

The eighth lot fell to Abijah. From this class came Zechariah, the father of John the Baptist; hence it is certain that Zechariah was not the supreme High Priest, for the High Priest had no assigned turn but presided over all. Whether Zechariah was a pontiff (i.e., the chief and firstborn of his family of Abijah), as St. Dionysius holds (Celestial Hierarchy, ch. 4), and whether he was from the descendants of Eleazar or from the descendants of Ithamar, remains uncertain.
The son of Uzziah: Benno — and the others who are enumerated in verse 27, as will be noted there.
Son of Merari: Uzziah — these words seem to be taken as a parenthesis, for the following names belong to a separate listing.

The Levites also cast lots against their brethren the sons of Aaron, before King David, Zadok, Ahimelech, and the chiefs of the priestly and Levitical families — the lot divided all equally, both the greater and the lesser.

Those who "prophesied" (that is, who sang and praised God) on citharas, according to their number, serving in the office dedicated to them — meaning each family sang in their turn with as many singers as had been appointed for singing; for a larger family forming one choir-order had more singers than a smaller one with fewer members. The Hebrew reads "skilled men in their service," that is, diligent men, expertly performing their duty. As Vatablus says, all these were most skilled musicians.

Under the hand (under the governance and direction) of Asaph "prophesying" (singing God's praises) "beside the king" — that is, in the citadel of Zion before the ark, near the seat of David, where David was accustomed to sit and attend divine praises. For it is likely that other singers were assigned to sing in the Tabernacle of Moses at Gibeon; hence some, to encompass both groups, explain "beside the king" as meaning according to the king's prescription and distribution, so that some sang in Zion, others in Gibeon.

The sons of Jeduthun — Gedaliah, Zeri, Jeshaiah, Hashabiah, and Mattithiah — six. Only five are enumerated here, as is clear to anyone counting. The sixth is therefore missing, and it is probable that he is Shemaiah, who is named at v. 17 in the tenth lot; for Shemaiah is not named among the sons of Asaph nor among the sons of Heman. He was therefore a son of Jeduthun, whose sons are described here along with those of the other two, as is evident from v. 1.

All these were sons of Heman, the king's seer in the words of God. Heman is called "seer," that is, a prophet — that is, a singer of divine praises before David. "In the words of God" means that sacred words and praises were to be sung; David had in his court and at table his own musicians who sang secular pieces for the king's honorable recreation, but Heman took care that only sacred music was sung in the Tabernacle when David was engaged in divine worship. The "words of God" were therefore psalms, hymns, and canticles of divine praise.

To lift up the horn — that is, by singing to exalt the greatness, magnificence, and immense majesty, wisdom, mercy, power, clemency, and beneficence of God (so Abulensis). More precisely, Heman took care that psalms be sung in which David prays and foretells the exaltation of the horn — that is, the kingdom, power, and glory both of his own reign and of the antitype Christ. For David found this most gratifying and most longed for. Hence he frequently treats this exaltation of the horn in the psalms. Vatablus thus translates: "who was the king's seer in divine words, so that the horn might be raised," explaining this as Heman prophesying that David would attain great fame and glory; for "horn" signifies dignity and glory, likewise strength and power — as if to say, for exalting the royal dignity and power. For Asaph and Jeduthun sang other psalms directed toward instructing and consoling the people, and stirring them to piety and to praising God together.
Who were instructed in singing — that is, teachers, as follows; who taught others to sing and play skillfully, both with voice and with musical instruments.

The learned equally with the unlearned — that is, equally the skilled and the unskilled in singing, equally teacher and pupil — meaning David distributed the singers into 24 classes and gave each class twelve masters skilled in music to teach the others to sing, to direct and perfect them in song. Therefore these masters were in total 288, as stated in v. 7. Multiply 24 by 12 and the number 288 results.

The divisions of the gatekeepers: from the Korahites, Meshelemiah son of Kore, from the sons of Asaph. The orders and classes of the Levites who were doorkeepers of the Temple are here enumerated, along with those entrusted with guarding the treasuries both of the Temple and of the king.
The sons of Obed-edom: Shemaiah the firstborn, Jozabad the second, Joah the third, Sachar the fourth, Nathanael the fifth.

Ammiel the sixth, Issachar the seventh, Peullethai the eighth — because the Lord blessed him. That is, God so blessed Obed-edom with offspring that he had eight sons, all suitable for service, in addition to sixty-two descendants noted in v. 8.
To Shemaiah his son were born sons who ruled over their family's household, for they were very mighty men.
All these of the sons of Obed-edom, they and their sons and their brothers, mighty men fit for service — sixty-two from Obed-edom.
The sons of Meshelemiah and their brothers — strong men — eighteen in total.
Of Hosah, from the sons of Merari: Shimri the chief (for he had no firstborn, and therefore his father made him chief).
These were divided as gatekeepers so that the chief men of the watches, like their brothers, would continually minister in the house of the Lord.
Lots were cast equally for small and great alike, by their families, for each of the gates.
The lot for the east fell to Shelemiah. Then for Zechariah his son, a most prudent and learned man, the lot for the north fell.
Obed-edom and his sons received the south, where the council-house of the elders was located.
Shuppim and Hosah received the west, by the gate that leads up the ascent road — watch opposite watch.
On the east, six Levites; on the north, four each day; on the south likewise four each day; and where the council met, two and two.
In the storerooms of the gatekeepers on the west, four on the road and two in each storeroom.
Ahijah was over the treasuries of the house of God and the vessels of the holy things.
The sons of Jehieli — Zethan and Joel his brothers — were over the treasuries of the house of the Lord.
Shebuel son of Gershom, son of Moses, was chief overseer of the treasuries.

Shelomith himself and his brothers were over the treasuries of the holy things which King David had consecrated, along with the heads of families, the commanders of thousands and hundreds, and the captains of the army.
These were taken from the wars and from the spoils of battles, which had been consecrated for the restoration and furnishing of the Temple of the Lord.

All these things which Samuel the seer, and Saul son of Kish, and Abner son of Ner, and Joab son of Zeruiah had consecrated — all who had consecrated anything — were in the care of Shelomith and his brothers.
Over the Izharites, Conaniah and his sons were appointed for external affairs over Israel, to teach and judge them.
Of the Hebronites, Hashabiah and his brothers — 1,700 mighty men — had oversight over Israel west of the Jordan, in all the work of the Lord and in the service of the king.
The chief of the Hebronites was Jeriah, according to their genealogical records.

The sons of Israel according to their number — their family heads, tribunes, centurions, and prefects who ministered to the king in their divisions, entering and departing month by month throughout the year, each division consisting of twenty-four thousand. Note: Just as David in the two preceding chapters distributed priests and Levites into classes to serve God in the temple by weekly rotation, so here he distributes the remaining people — namely the Israelites fit for war — into 12 classes, appointing a prince over each, so that each class in turn might serve the king and commonwealth in military matters for one month each year, meaning 24,000 armed men with their commander would be ready at all times for any military expedition. This was a politically wise ordinance of David: both to exercise all his subjects in arms so they might learn to make war, and for the protection of himself and his kingdom. Since the Israelites, being few, were surrounded on all sides by enemies — Philistines, Edomites, Ammonites, Arabs, Egyptians — they needed always to be prepared for war. Lest they be overwhelmed by a sudden enemy incursion, David established that 24,000 men should always stand under arms as a guard for the king and kingdom, ready to meet attackers at once. This was not burdensome to the subjects, since each was required to take the field for only one month per year, and in the remaining months attended to his own affairs at home. Moreover, by this arrangement enormous monetary levies — normally imposed for recruiting and maintaining an army — were avoided, since each man sustained himself during his own month of service.
The first division for the first month: Jesboam son of Zabdiel was in command, with twenty-four thousand under him.
Of the sons of Phares, the chief of all the commanders of the army for the first month.
The second month's division: Dudia the Ahohite, and after him another named Macelloth, who governed a portion of the army of twenty-four thousand.
The commander of the third division for the third month was Banaias son of Joiada the priest, with twenty-four thousand in his division.
This is the Banaias who was the mightiest of the thirty and was set over the thirty; his son Amizabad commanded his division.
The fourth, for the fourth month: Azahel brother of Joab, and Zabadias his son after him, with twenty-four thousand in his division.
David did not wish to number those twenty years old and under, because the Lord had said He would multiply Israel as the stars of heaven.

Joab son of Sarvia had begun to number but did not finish, because wrath had fallen upon Israel on account of this; and therefore the number of those who had been counted was not recorded in the chronicles of King David.
Over the king's treasuries was Azmoth son of Adiel. Over the treasuries in the cities, villages, and towers, Jonathan son of Ozias presided.
Over the agricultural work and the farmers who tilled the land, Ezri son of Chelub was in charge.
Over the vinedressers, Semeias the Romathite; over the wine cellars, Zabdias the Aphonite.
Over the olive groves and fig orchards in the plains, Balanan the Gederite; over the oil stores, Joas.

Over the herds pasturing in Saron, Setrai the Saronite was appointed; over the cattle in the valleys, Saphat son of Adli. Lapide notes: "Ad opera forinsecus" refers to external works such as cutting and polishing timber and stone, cultivating the vineyards, meadows, and fields of the temple, etc.
Over the camels, Ubil the Ishmaelite; over the donkeys, Jadias the Meronathite.
Over the sheep, Jaziz the Agarene. All these were princes of the substance of King David.
Jonathan, David's uncle, was a counselor, a prudent and learned man; he and Jahiel son of Hachamoni were with the king's sons.
Achitophel also was the king's counselor, and Chusai the Arachite was the king's friend.
After Achitophel came Joiada son of Banaias, and Abiathar. The commander of the king's army was Joab.

In the part of the house where the council of the elders [Sanhedrim] was located — that is, in the southern part of the temple there was the place of the supreme council, called the Sanhedrin, composed of 72 elders serving as senators. See the notes on Numbers 11.

Which leads to the way of the ascent — namely the way by which one ascended to Mount Zion and to the tabernacle erected there, and thereafter to the temple built on the same site by Solomon. "Guard opposite guard" [Vatable: "a guard over against a guard"]: one watch of guards answered to another placed opposite it. This western gate of the temple appears to have had a double guard more than the others, because of the greater frequency of people there, and consequently the greater danger of disorder. This is confirmed by verse 18.

At the western gate, in the gatekeepers' cells in the road, four Levite chiefs of the gatekeepers kept watch on the road of ascent mentioned in verse 16; and furthermore two other chiefs distributed through the appointed cells kept watch with the lesser Levites subject to them. Hence it is clear that the guards at the western gate were more numerous and greater than at the other gates of the temple.

David convoked all the princes of Israel, the leaders of the tribes, the commanders of divisions who served the king, the tribunes and centurions, those who oversaw the king's property and possessions, his sons with the eunuchs, and the most powerful and mighty men in the army at Jerusalem.

On "with the eunuchs": eunuchs were formerly in charge of royal courts, especially the women's quarters (gyneceum); hence any prefects of the court or palace officials were called eunuchs, as Lapide noted at Genesis 37:36.

And the footstool of our God: this is what the Ark of the Covenant is called, because the Cherubim standing above the Ark with joined wings provided as it were a throne for God, so that the Ark beneath was like a footstool for His feet, as explained at Exodus 25.
God said to David: "You shall not build a house for my name, because you are a man of war and have shed blood." David humbly accepts this divine prohibition, explaining it to the assembly.

For of Judah He chose princes: the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Septuagint have the singular "a prince," but this means a prince — that is, princes succeeding one another in an unbroken line — according to Genesis 49:10: "The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor a ruler from his descendants, until he comes who is to be sent," namely the Messiah or Christ, who was to be born of Judah and David.
God declared: "Solomon your son shall build my house and my courts; for I have chosen him to be my son, and I will be his father."

And I will establish his kingdom forever, if he perseveres in keeping my commandments and judgments, as he does today. The eternity of the kingdom is conditional upon Solomon's obedience — ultimately fulfilled in Christ, the true eternal King.

David exhorts the whole assembly of Israel, in the hearing of God, to keep and seek out all the commandments of the Lord, so that they may possess the good land and leave it to their children after them forever.

But you, Solomon my son, know the God of your father and serve Him with a perfect heart and a willing mind. To "know" God means to acknowledge and recognize Him — to believe firmly, and often to think upon and meditate on the immense majesty, power, justice, and goodness of God, so that this knowledge and frequent meditation may impel one to worship, fear, and love Him continually. # GEM

For the Lord searches all hearts and understands every intent of the mind. If you seek Him, you will find Him; but if you forsake Him, He will reject you forever. A powerful warning: the sincerity of interior religion is demanded, not mere outward observance.
Now therefore, since the Lord has chosen you to build a house for the sanctuary, be strong and do it. David encourages Solomon with the divine election itself as the basis for confidence.

David gave Solomon his son the plan of the porch and of the temple, of the storerooms, the upper rooms, the inner chambers, and the house of propitiation — that is, the Holy of Holies; for in it was the propitiatory (mercy seat) above the ark, from which God, being propitious, gave oracles to the High Priest and bestowed benefits upon the people.

David gave Solomon the plans of all the courts and the surrounding chambers, for the treasuries of the house of the Lord and for the treasuries of the holy things — meaning the treasuries (gazophylacia) or storehouses in which the money offered and consecrated (i.e., dedicated) to God was kept.
And for the divisions of the priests and the Levites: by this expression David designates the 24 classes into which the priests and Levites had been divided, as discussed in chapters 23 and 24.

The golden lions and silver lions: no mention of these small lions (leunculi) and larger lions is made in the description of the Temple in 3 Kings (1 Kings), which shows that David's plan contained details beyond what is recorded elsewhere.

For the altar of incense, David assigned the purest gold, so that from it might be fashioned the likeness of a chariot of Cherubim spreading their wings and covering the Ark of the Covenant of the Lord.

All this, said David, "came to me in writing from the hand of the Lord, so that I might understand all the works of the pattern." David declares that the entire architectural plan of the Temple was divinely revealed to him by God — a heavenly blueprint communicated by inspiration, analogous to the pattern shown to Moses on Sinai.

David said to Solomon: "Be strong and courageous and do it. Do not be afraid or dismayed, for the Lord God, my God, is with you. He will not fail you or forsake you, until you have finished all the work for the service of the house of the Lord."

Behold, the divisions of the priests and the Levites for all the service of the house of God stand ready to assist you; and the princes and all the people are entirely at your command. David assures Solomon that every human resource has been organized and prepared for the great undertaking.

Concerning the altar on which incense was burned (namely the altar of incense, which stood in the Holy Place facing the Holy of Holies), David gave the purest gold, so that from it might be made the likeness of a chariot of the Cherubim spreading their wings and covering the ark of the covenant of the Lord (so the Hebrew, Septuagint, and Latin Vulgate agree). One may ask: what was this chariot of the Cherubim, and of what kind? Was it the same as the Mosaic and Solomonic Cherubim described in 3 Kings chapters 7 and 8? Jerome addresses this question. The Septuagint, Vatablus, Pagninus, and Abulensis render the Hebrew word 'kephir' (meaning a young lion) as 'cappor,' translating it as bowls or cups, indicating their proposed use.

Lapide refutes Prado's interpretation that Solomon made a "quadriga" (four-horse chariot) from Moses' two Cherubim by adding two more. Lapide argues this is an entirely different set of Cherubim, distinct from both Moses' original two and Solomon's additional pair in 3 Kings 7–8. His first argument: Moses' Cherubim stood beside the Ark in the Holy of Holies, whereas these were made in the Holy Place above the altar of incense. Second: Scripture says Solomon made them "in the likeness," implying similarity, not identity, with the Ark's Cherubim. Third: Solomon's Cherubim added to Moses' did not form a quadriga or unified structure — the Mosaic ones faced each other with wings spread over the Ark, while Solomon's stood to the side, each wing spanning the full length of the Holy of Holies touching north and south walls respectively, and thus could only form one side of a chariot, not a complete quadriga. Fourth: Scripture nowhere mentions a wheeled cart upon which the Ark rested, as Prado claims. Lapide concludes that Cherubim were fashioned not only around the Ark but also on the altar of incense, and these latter are the subject here. As the former veiled the Ark with joined wings, so these veiled the altar of incense with joined wings, providing as it were a throne for God, who seated upon them appeared to receive the incense smoke offered on the altar. The Hebrew word merkabah means "chariot" not because there were four Cherubim, but because the two Cherubim, facing one another with two wings spread and joined to the wings of the other, formed a square or quadrilateral shape — thus a "four-square chariot." So Abulensis. The sense therefore is: David gave the purest gold to make the altar of incense and upon it the chariot of the Cherubim, similar to the chariot of the Cherubim veiling the Ark of the Covenant made by Moses. Lapide notes he treated the literal and mystical significance of the Cherubim extensively in his commentaries on Exodus 25, Ezekiel 1, and Apocalypse 4.

On "All this came to me written by the hand of the Lord, that I might understand all the works of the pattern": God inscribed the entire design of the Temple and delivered it through the hand of an Angel to David, so that David could hand it to Solomon to build accordingly. Lapide compares this to God's delivery of the tabernacle plan to Moses at Sinai (Exodus 25:40): "See that you make them after the pattern shown to you on the mountain." The "pattern" or paradigm (as the Septuagint renders it) denotes both the ideal blueprint and its literal verbal explanation, since the design alone, being obscure and complex, could scarcely have been understood by David from mere visual inspection. Therefore God also explained it in written words, specifying in explicit letters: "Thus shall you make the Holy Place, thus the Holy of Holies, thus the court, thus each altar, thus the lampstand, thus the table of showbread" — each with its material, form, and dimensions. This is what David means by "they came written by the hand of the Lord to me, that I might understand all the works of the pattern." So Abulensis.

David addresses the whole assembly, declaring that Solomon alone has been chosen by God, though still a boy and tender in years. The work is great, says David, "for the habitation is prepared not for man, but for God." Lapide notes the humility and faith of David, who acknowledges that even a king's dwelling pales before the house of the Lord.

David enumerates the vast materials he has personally prepared with all his strength for the house of God: gold for golden vessels, silver for silver, bronze for bronze, iron for iron, wood for wooden works, onyx stones, stones like stibium (antimony), stones of diverse colors, every precious stone, and Parian marble in great abundance. Lapide observes that the variety and costliness of these materials reflects the glory due to God's dwelling.

Beyond the public treasury, David adds from his own private fortune (peculio suo) gold and silver for the temple, over and above what he had already prepared for the holy precincts. Lapide commends this as a model of royal generosity, distinguishing between public resources and personal sacrifice freely given.

David specifies: three thousand talents of gold from the gold of Ophir, and seven thousand talents of the finest silver, designated for overlaying the walls of the temple. Lapide notes the immensity of this sum as evidence of David's total devotion, and cites patristic writers who see in Ophir gold a figure of the most pure and tested virtue.

David invites all who are willing to consecrate their hands (impleat manum suam) and offer freely to the Lord whatever they wish. Lapide interprets "filling the hand" as an ancient idiom for consecrating oneself to sacred service, and sees in this voluntary offering a figure of Christian almsgiving and the free sacrifice of the will to God. # GEM

The princes of the families, the leaders of the tribes of Israel, the commanders of thousands and of hundreds, and the overseers of the king's works all responded with generous pledges. Lapide notes this unanimous response as reflecting the unity of the people around the sacred work, a model for the Church when undertaking great works for God's glory.
